DOE BER Research Programs

Bioenergy Research Centers, Scientific Focus Areas, AI Projects, and User Facilities

Programs Variables Variable index YAML
Bioenergy Research Centers (4)
GLBRC Great Lakes Bioenergy Research Center

GLBRC is a cross-disciplinary research center developing sustainable biofuels and bioproducts from dedicated energy crops grown on marginal, nonagricultural lands. Research spans three crosscutting themes: sustainable bioenergy cropping systems, sustainable biomass conversion, and sustainable field-to-product optimization.

Lead institution: University of Wisconsin-Madison
Partners:
Michigan State University Doe Joint Genome Institute Pacific Northwest National Laboratory
Location: Madison, Wisconsin
Funding: DOE BER / Genomic Science Program (2023-2028) - $375 million (across three funding phases since 2007)
Start date: 2007
Established: 2007
Principal investigators:
Timothy Donohue
Director
University of Wisconsin-Madison
Federica Brandizzi
Science Director
Michigan State University
Links:
Main Doe Page Data Portal Atlas
NMDC umbrella study: nmdc:sty-11-y1kdd163
Scientific questions:
  • How can we develop sustainable bioenergy cropping systems on marginal lands?
  • What are the mechanisms of lignocellulose deconstruction?
  • How do we optimize conversion of biomass to fuels and chemicals?
  • What microorganisms and enzymes can efficiently convert plant biomass?
  • How do we balance productivity with ecosystem sustainability?
Keywords:
Bioenergy Biofuels Lignocellulose Switchgrass Poplar Energy Sorghum Miscanthus Microbial Conversion Sustainability Marginal Lands Cellulosic Biomass Lignin Valorization
Data types:
Genomics Transcriptomics Metabolomics Proteomics Field Trial Data Satellite Imagery Crop Yield Data Soil Microbiome Data Enzyme Activity Data
Modalities:
Field Experiments Greenhouse Studies Laboratory Fermentation Remote Sensing Long Term Ecological Research High Throughput Screening
Field Sites 1
W.K. Kellogg Biological Station (Hickory Corners, Michigan)

Michigan State University long-term ecological research site. Sarah Evans (MSU) is studying how agricultural management affects soil molecular properties, linking soil measurements to management decisions (tillage, cover crops, perennials) and ecosystem service outcomes. Summer 2024 campaign.

Type: Lter
PI: Sarah Evans
Institution: Michigan State University
Relevance:
Biotech Agriculture
Programs: Great Lakes Bioenergy Research Center, Molecular Observation Network
Studies 11
nmdc:sty-11-e4yb9z58 - Seasonal activities of the phyllosphere microbiome of perennial crops (Ashley Shade)

Genome-centric approach to identify ecologically important leaf microbiome members on switchgrass and miscanthus, quantifying activities over two growing seasons. Classes represented by MAGs (Actinomycetia, Alpha- and Gamma-Proteobacteria, Bacteroidota) are active in late season with stress-associated pathways and biosynthetic pathways for terpenes and non-ribosomal peptides.

Organism: switchgrass phyllosphere metagenome
Data modalities:
Metagenomics Metatranscriptomics
Primary reference: doi:10.1038/s41467-023-36515-y - Seasonal activities of the phyllosphere microbiome of perennial crops
Study Design
Design type: time series design OBI:0500020
Observational units: phyllosphere microbiome samples
Sampling strategy: Seasonal field sampling over two growing seasons from perennial crop phyllospheres.
Replication: Samples collected across switchgrass and miscanthus field plots.
Notes: Initial variable catalog entry; source field names should be refined against the raw submission.
Study Variables 29
Variable Roles Value Context Mappings
sampling season
Seasonal sampling point for phyllosphere microbiome activity measurements.

Categorical

Time series yes; seasonal sampling over two growing seasons
Fields season, sampling_date

nmdc:sty-11-wbc14h22 - Switchgrass cropping systems on marginal lands (Maren Friesen)

Understanding how rhizosphere microbiome influences soil nitrogen transformations under low- and high-N conditions across five locations in the Great Lakes Region.

Organism: Panicum virgatum
Data modalities:
Amplicon 16s
Samples: 2971
BioProjects: PRJNA733109, PRJNA733505, PRJNA733764
Primary reference: doi:10.1186/s40793-023-00504-x - Regional biogeography versus intra-annual dynamics of the root and soil microbiome
Source: Regional biogeography versus intra-annual dynamics of the root and soil microbiome
GLBRC data page linking the switchgrass microbiome publication, NCBI sequence data, analysis code, and processed data resources.
Study Design
Design type: study design OBI:0500000
Experimental units: switchgrass field plots
Observational units: rhizosphere soil samples
Sampling strategy: Field sampling across low- and high-nitrogen switchgrass cropping systems.
Replication: Samples span five Great Lakes region field locations.
Notes: Initial variable catalog entry based on study description.
Study Variables 22
Variable Roles Value Context Mappings
nitrogen treatment
Low- versus high-nitrogen cropping system condition for switchgrass plots.
Experimental Factor Study Design Independent Variable
Categorical
Levels low nitrogen, high nitrogen
Entity switchgrass cropping system
Fields nitrogen_treatment, n_treatment, fertilizer_regimen
BERVO total fertilizer N amendment BERVO:0000895
MIxS fertilizer regimen MIXS:0000556
MIxS experimental factor MIXS:0000008

Fields location, site, field_site

HIGH Sorghum Phyllosphere Microbiome (Ashley Shade)

Functional and genomic diversity of the sorghum phyllosphere microbiome. Collection of 47 bacteria isolated from aerial root mucilage of energy sorghum plus metagenome and metatranscriptome data from phyllosphere communities.

Organism: Sorghum bicolor
Data modalities:
Metagenomics Metatranscriptomics Amplicon 16s
Keywords:
Sorghum Rhizosphere Plant Microbe Interactions Bioenergy
BioProjects: PRJNA862978, PRJNA844896
BRC Dataset IDs: Gs0157305, PRJNA862978, PRJNA844896, 503249
Primary reference: doi:10.1038/s41467-024-50463-1 - Disentangling plant- and environment-mediated drivers of active rhizosphere bacterial community dynamics during short-term drought
Study Design
Design type: study design OBI:0500000
Observational units: sorghum phyllosphere metagenome samples, sorghum phyllosphere metatranscriptome samples, sorghum-associated bacterial isolates
Sampling strategy: Genome-resolved and transcript-resolved profiling of sorghum-associated phyllosphere communities, paired with isolation of culturable aerial root mucilage bacteria.
Notes: Variable catalog entry based on BRC dataset metadata and primary reference.
Study Variables 4
Variable Roles Value Context Mappings
host crop
Sorghum host associated with phyllosphere, aerial root mucilage, or rhizosphere samples.
Study Design Independent Variable Grouping
Categorical
Entity Sorghum bicolor
Fields host_common_name, crop, host_species
MIxS host common name MIXS:0000248
plant-associated compartment
Sorghum plant-associated habitat sampled for community profiling or isolate recovery.
Study Design Independent Variable Grouping
Categorical
Levels phyllosphere, aerial root mucilage, rhizosphere
Entity sorghum-associated microbial habitat
Fields compartment, sample_type, niche
MIxS local environmental context MIXS:0000013
MIxS environmental medium MIXS:0000014
sorghum-associated microbial community composition
Amplicon, metagenomic, or metatranscriptomic profiles of sorghum-associated microbial communities.
Study Design Dependent Variable Measured Variable
Array
Entity sorghum-associated microbial community
Matrix phyllosphere, aerial root mucilage, and rhizosphere
Method amplicon sequencing, metagenomics, and metatranscriptomics
Fields asv_table, metagenome_profile, transcript_abundance
bacterial isolate identity
Identity of culturable sorghum-associated bacteria isolated from aerial root mucilage.
Identifier Measured Variable
Identifier
Entity sorghum-associated bacterial isolate
Method bacterial isolation and genome sequencing
Fields isolate_id, strain_id, genome_accession
HIGH Switchgrass Root and Soil Microbiome (Lukas P. Bell-Dereske)

Variation in root and soil microbiome over space and time in switchgrass. Bacterial and fungal community characterization from GLBRC Marginal Lands Experiment via 16S and ITS amplicon sequencing.

Organism: Panicum virgatum
Data modalities:
Amplicon 16s Amplicon Its
Keywords:
Switchgrass Rhizosphere Soil Microbiome Marginal Lands Bacterial Fungal Interactions
BioProjects: PRJNA733764, PRJNA799201
BRC Dataset IDs: PRJNA733764, PRJNA799201, zenodo.7307179, 10.17605/OSF.IO/5VW9C
Primary reference: doi:10.1186/s40793-023-00504-x - Regional biogeography versus intra-annual dynamics of the root and soil microbiome
Study Design
Design type: study design OBI:0500000
Experimental units: switchgrass monoculture plots
Observational units: root samples, bulk soil samples
Sampling strategy: Regional sampling across five GLBRC Marginal Land Experiment sites plus repeated seasonal sampling at Lux Arbor.
Replication: Field plots sampled across Michigan and Wisconsin sites with repeated root and soil collections.
Notes: Variable catalog entry based on the primary publication and BRC study description.
Study Variables 5
Variable Roles Value Context Mappings
sampling site
GLBRC Marginal Land Experiment site where switchgrass root or soil samples were collected.
Study Design Independent Variable Spatial Index Grouping
Categorical
Levels Lake City, Escanaba, Lux Arbor, Hancock, Rhinelander
Entity field site
Fields site, location, field_site
MIxS local environmental context MIXS:0000013
sample compartment
Belowground habitat sampled for switchgrass-associated microbiome profiling.
Study Design Independent Variable Grouping
Categorical
Levels root, bulk soil
Entity switchgrass-associated microbial habitat
Matrix root and bulk soil
Fields compartment, sample_type, niche
MIxS local environmental context MIXS:0000013
MIxS environmental medium MIXS:0000014
collection date
Date or seasonal sampling point used to evaluate intra-annual microbiome dynamics.
Date Time Variable Study Design Independent Variable
Datetime
Entity sampling event
Time series yes; biweekly soil and monthly root sampling during the 2018 growing season
Fields collection_date, sampling_date, date
MIxS collection date MIXS:0000011
MIxS season MIXS:0000829
nitrogen addition
Nitrogen addition treatment evaluated as a possible driver of switchgrass microbiome structure.
Experimental Factor Study Design Independent Variable
Categorical
Levels no nitrogen addition, nitrogen addition
Entity switchgrass field plot
Fields nitrogen_addition, n_addition, fertilizer_regimen
BERVO total fertilizer N amendment BERVO:0000895
MIxS fertilizer regimen MIXS:0000556
MIxS experimental factor MIXS:0000008
bacterial and fungal community composition
Amplicon-derived bacterial and fungal community profiles from switchgrass roots and soils.
Study Design Dependent Variable Measured Variable
Array
Entity switchgrass-associated microbial community
Matrix root and bulk soil
Method 16S rRNA and ITS amplicon sequencing
Fields otu_table, asv_table, bacterial_community_profile, fungal_community_profile
MEDIUM Transgenic Switchgrass Microbiome Effects (Greg Bonito)

Effects of genetic modification of the shikimate pathway (QsuB transgenic) on switchgrass-associated microbiomes in different plant compartments. 16S and ITS rDNA sequencing of roots, leaves, and soil.

Organism: Panicum virgatum
Data modalities:
Amplicon 16s Amplicon Its
Keywords:
Switchgrass Lignin Plant Microbe Interactions Metabolic Engineering
BioProjects: PRJNA1002602, PRJNA1002603
BRC Dataset IDs: PRJNA1002602, PRJNA1002603
Primary reference: doi:10.1128/spectrum.01546-24 - Genetic modification of the shikimate pathway to reduce lignin content in switchgrass ( <i>Panicum virgatum</i> L.) significantly impacts plant microbiomes
Study Design
Design type: study design OBI:0500000
Experimental units: switchgrass plants
Observational units: plant compartment microbiome samples
Sampling strategy: Amplicon profiling of wild-type and QsuB transgenic switchgrass-associated compartments.
Controls: Wild-type switchgrass plants provide the comparison condition for QsuB transgenic plants.
Notes: Variable catalog entry based on the primary publication and BRC study description.
Study Variables 3
Variable Roles Value Context Mappings
QsuB transgenic status
Host plant engineering status for the shikimate pathway modification that reduces lignin content.
Experimental Factor Study Design Independent Variable
Categorical
Levels wild type, QsuB transgenic
Entity Panicum virgatum
Fields transgenic_status, genotype, qsub_status
MIxS host genotype MIXS:0000365
MIxS experimental factor MIXS:0000008
plant compartment
Switchgrass plant or soil compartment sampled for microbiome profiling.
Study Design Independent Variable Grouping
Categorical
Levels root, leaf, soil
Entity switchgrass-associated microbial habitat
Fields compartment, tissue, sample_type
MIxS local environmental context MIXS:0000013
MIxS environmental medium MIXS:0000014
bacterial and fungal community composition
16S and ITS amplicon profiles used to evaluate transgenic effects on switchgrass microbiomes.
Study Design Dependent Variable Measured Variable
Array
Entity switchgrass-associated microbial community
Matrix roots, leaves, and soil
Method 16S rRNA and ITS amplicon sequencing
Fields otu_table, asv_table, bacterial_community_profile, fungal_community_profile
MEDIUM Terpene Effects on Sorghum Microbiome (Greg Bonito)

Examining the effects of terpene addition on the microbial community in Sorghum belowground compartments via amplicon sequencing.

Organism: Sorghum bicolor
Data modalities:
Amplicon 16s
Keywords:
Sorghum Rhizosphere Plant Microbe Interactions Terpenes
Samples: 228
BioProjects: PRJNA933671
BRC Dataset IDs: PRJNA933671
Primary reference: doi:10.1128/spectrum.01332-23 - Terpenes modulate bacterial and fungal growth and sorghum rhizobiome communities
Study Design
Design type: study design OBI:0500000
Experimental units: rhizobox mesocosms
Observational units: sorghum belowground microbiome samples
Sampling strategy: Mesocosm experiment delivering terpene treatments through an artificial root system with pre-treatment and post-treatment microbiome sampling.
Controls: Untreated or solvent-control sorghum mesocosms used as treatment baselines.
Notes: Variable catalog entry based on the primary publication and BRC study description.
Study Variables 4
Variable Roles Value Context Mappings
terpene treatment
Exogenous terpene supplied belowground to test effects on sorghum rhizobiome communities.
Experimental Factor Study Design Independent Variable
Categorical
Levels control, 1,8-cineole, linalool, nerolidol
Entity sorghum rhizosphere
Fields terpene, terpene_treatment, chemical_administration
MIxS chemical administration MIXS:0000751
MIxS experimental factor MIXS:0000008
terpene dose
Concentration or dose level for exogenous terpene delivery.
Study Design Independent Variable
Numeric
Entity terpene amendment
Fields dose, concentration, terpene_concentration
MIxS chemical administration MIXS:0000751
sampling time point
Sampling point relative to terpene application and plant growth.
Date Time Variable Study Design Independent Variable
Categorical
Entity sampling event
Time series yes; pre-treatment and post-treatment belowground sampling
Fields time_point, sampling_time, collection_date
MIxS collection date MIXS:0000011
belowground microbiome composition
Bacterial and fungal community profiles from sorghum root-associated belowground compartments.
Study Design Dependent Variable Measured Variable
Array
Entity sorghum rhizobiome
Matrix root-associated soil and rhizosphere compartments
Method 16S rRNA and ITS amplicon sequencing
Fields bacterial_community_profile, fungal_community_profile, otu_table, asv_table
HIGH KBS MCSE Long-term Cropping Experiment Metagenomes (Jennifer M. Jones)

Metagenomics from Kellogg Biological Station Long Term Ecological Research Main Cropping System Experiment (KBS MCSE). Testing how drought affects microbial allocation to stress tolerance across different cropping systems.

Organism: soil metagenome
Data modalities:
Metagenomics
Keywords:
Soil Microbiome Drought Carbon Use Efficiency Long Term Ecological Research
Samples: 106
Sites: W.K. Kellogg Biological Station
BioProjects: PRJNA1156252
BRC Dataset IDs: PRJNA1156252, knb-lter-kbs.238.1
Primary reference: doi:10.1016/j.soilbio.2025.109787 - Drought increases microbial allocation to stress tolerance but with few tradeoffs among community-level traits
Study Design
Design type: study design OBI:0500000
Experimental units: KBS Main Cropping System Experiment plots
Observational units: soil metagenome samples
Sampling strategy: Long-term field experiment sampling across drought and cropping-system treatments at Kellogg Biological Station.
Notes: Variable catalog entry based on study description and primary reference.
Study Variables 4
Variable Roles Value Context Mappings
drought treatment
Field drought manipulation used to test microbial allocation to stress tolerance.
Experimental Factor Study Design Independent Variable
Categorical
Entity cropping-system field plot
Fields drought_treatment, water_treatment, perturbation
MIxS perturbation MIXS:0000754
MIxS watering regimen MIXS:0000591
MIxS experimental factor MIXS:0000008

Long-term KBS MCSE cropping-system treatment represented by each soil sample.
Study Design Independent Variable Grouping
Categorical
Entity agricultural field plot
Fields cropping_system, crop_rotation, treatment
MIxS local environmental context MIXS:0000013
microbial stress tolerance allocation
Metagenome-derived community trait allocation to stress tolerance under drought.
Study Design Dependent Variable Derived
Array
Entity soil microbial community trait profile
Matrix soil
Method metagenomic trait inference
Fields stress_tolerance_genes, trait_allocation, functional_profile
soil metagenome profile
Taxonomic and functional composition of soil microbial communities in KBS MCSE plots.
Study Design Dependent Variable Measured Variable
Array
Entity soil microbial community
Matrix soil
Method metagenomics
Fields metagenome_profile, taxonomic_profile, functional_profile
MIxS environmental medium MIXS:0000014
MEDIUM Soil Pore Space Decomposition Microbiome (Gian Maria Niccolò Benucci)

Exploring interactions between plant detritus decomposition and micro-scale characteristics of soil physical micro-environments. Soil metagenome sequencing of leaf litter decomposition in soil microcosms.

Organism: soil metagenome
Data modalities:
Metagenomics
Keywords:
Soil Microbiome Decomposition Carbon Cycling
Samples: 872
BioProjects: PRJNA938072
BRC Dataset IDs: PRJNA938072
Primary reference: doi:10.3389/fmicb.2023.1172862 - The microbiome structure of decomposing plant leaves in soil depends on plant species, soil pore sizes, and soil moisture content
Study Design
Design type: study design OBI:0500000
Experimental units: soil microcosms
Observational units: decomposing leaf litter soil metagenome samples
Sampling strategy: Soil microcosm experiment varying plant detritus, soil pore size, and soil moisture.
Notes: Variable catalog entry based on the primary publication title and study description.

Variable Roles Value Context Mappings
leaf litter plant species
Plant species identity of decomposing leaf detritus placed into soil microcosms.
Experimental Factor Study Design Independent Variable
Categorical
Entity decomposing plant detritus
Fields plant_species, litter_species, host_common_name
MIxS host common name MIXS:0000248
MIxS experimental factor MIXS:0000008

Experimental Factor Study Design Independent Variable
Categorical
Entity soil physical microenvironment
Fields pore_size, soil_pore_size, physical_microenvironment
MIxS experimental factor MIXS:0000008
soil moisture content

Experimental Factor Covariate Measured Variable
Numeric
Entity soil water
Matrix soil
Fields soil_moisture, water_content
BERVO total soil water content BERVO:0000301

Array

Method metagenomics
Fields metagenome_profile, taxonomic_profile, functional_profile
MIxS environmental medium MIXS:0000014
LOW Bioreactor Fermentation Microbiomes (Daniel R. Noguera)

Metagenome-assembled genomes from anaerobic bioreactor microbial communities fermenting dairy coproducts (UltraFiltered Milk Permeate and Cottage cheese Acid Whey). Characterization of carboxylate platform fermentation systems.

Organism: bioreactor metagenome
Data modalities:
Metagenomics
Keywords:
Microbial Conversion Biofuels
BioProjects: PRJNA1159295, PRJNA1040840, PRJNA768492
BRC Dataset IDs: PRJNA1159295, PRJNA1040840, PRJNA768492
Primary reference: doi:10.3389/fbioe.2023.1173656 - A metagenome-level analysis of a microbial community fermenting ultra-filtered milk permeate
Study Design
Design type: study design OBI:0500000
Experimental units: anaerobic bioreactors
Observational units: bioreactor metagenome samples
Sampling strategy: Longitudinal anaerobic bioreactor sampling during fermentation of dairy coproducts.
Notes: Variable catalog entry based on study description and primary reference.
Study Variables 4
Variable Roles Value Context Mappings
dairy coproduct feedstock
Dairy coproduct used as fermentation substrate.
Experimental Factor Study Design Independent Variable
Categorical
Levels ultrafiltered milk permeate, cottage cheese acid whey
Entity bioreactor feedstock
Fields feedstock, substrate, dairy_coproduct
MIxS experimental factor MIXS:0000008
fermentation time point
Sampling point during anaerobic fermentation operation.
Date Time Variable Study Design Independent Variable
Datetime
Entity bioreactor sampling event
Time series yes
Fields time_point, day, collection_date
MIxS collection date MIXS:0000011
carboxylate product profile
Fermentation product profile for carboxylate platform compounds.
Study Design Dependent Variable Measured Variable
Array
Entity fermentation products

Bioenergy Microbial Conversion Omics Methods

Populus Variovorax SynCom28 Compartment Partitioning (Ashley Shade)

Defined 28-strain synthetic community of Variovorax isolates from Populus deltoides and Populus trichocarpa roots used to identify strain-level partitioning between rhizosphere and endosphere compartments and genomic traits linked to endosphere specialization.

Organism: Populus deltoides
Data modalities:
Metagenomics Genomics
BioProjects: PRJNA1322484
Source: Metagenomic sequencing of root-associated Variovorax synthetic communities in Populus
NCBI BioProject record for the Populus Variovorax SynCom28 metagenomic sequencing dataset, including rhizosphere and endosphere compartment metadata.
Study Design
Design type: study design OBI:0500000
Experimental units: Populus plants inoculated with SynCom28
Observational units: rhizosphere compartment samples, endosphere compartment samples
Sampling strategy: Defined 28-strain Variovorax synthetic community assay across Populus host species and root-associated compartments.
Notes: Variable catalog entry based on study description.

Variable Roles Value Context Mappings
Variovorax strain
Member strain of the 28-strain Populus Variovorax synthetic community.
Study Design Independent Variable Identifier
Identifier
Entity Variovorax strain
Fields strain_id, isolate_id, genome_accession
Populus host species
Populus host species used for synthetic community compartment partitioning assays.
Study Design Independent Variable Grouping
Categorical
Levels Populus deltoides, Populus trichocarpa
Entity Populus host plant
Fields host_species, host_common_name
MIxS host common name MIXS:0000248
root-associated compartment
Populus root-associated compartment where each Variovorax strain was quantified.
Study Design Independent Variable Grouping
Categorical
Levels rhizosphere, endosphere

Fields compartment, sample_type, niche
MIxS local environmental context MIXS:0000013
MIxS environmental medium MIXS:0000014
Map rhizosphere ENVO:00005801
strain compartment abundance
Strain-resolved abundance used to infer rhizosphere versus endosphere specialization.
Study Design Dependent Variable Measured Variable
Numeric
Entity Variovorax strain abundance
Matrix Populus rhizosphere and endosphere
Method strain-resolved metagenomics
Fields strain_abundance, relative_abundance, compartment_partitioning_score
Key Publications 15
doi:10.1007/s12155-015-9660-8 The DOE Bioenergy Research Centers: History, Operations, and Scientific Output
  • The four BRCs have produced over 1,800 peer-reviewed publications since 2007
  • Cross-center collaborations have enabled genome-to-field integration approaches
doi:10.1038/s41586-020-03127-1 Genomic mechanisms of climate adaptation in polyploid bioenergy switchgrass
  • High-quality genome assembly of 732 switchgrass genotypes reveals extensive genomic evidence of climate adaptation across 10 common gardens spanning 1,800 km latitude
  • Gene flow accelerated climate adaptation during postglacial colonization through introgression between divergent ecotypes
  • Polyploidy enhanced adaptive potential through fractionation of duplicated gene functions between upland and lowland ecotypes
  • Collaboration across all four DOE Bioenergy Research Centers
doi:10.1094/PBIOMES-07-20-0051-R
  • Strong temporal patterns in epiphytic microbial composition across growing season
  • Fungi-bacteria network associations were much more common than bacteria-bacteria associations
  • Identified core fungal taxa persistent throughout the growing season
doi:10.3390/agronomy13051294 Genome-Resolved Metagenomics of Nitrogen Transformations in the Switchgrass Rhizosphere Microbiome on Marginal Lands
  • Resolved 29 metagenome-assembled genomes from switchgrass rhizosphere on marginal lands
  • Rhizosphere microbiome may provide associative nitrogen fixation via novel diazotroph Janthinobacterium
  • Taxa from Verrucomicrobia, Acidobacteria, and Planctomycetes dominate nitrogen cycling in switchgrass rhizosphere
doi:10.1038/s42003-023-05290-3
  • Switchgrass produces bioactive terpenoids with antifungal properties
  • Ecotype-specific terpenoid profiles may shape distinct microbiome compositions
doi:10.1111/gcbb.13094 A 30% reduction in switchgrass rhizome reserves did not decrease biomass yield
  • Switchgrass can tolerate significant rhizome reserve reduction without yield loss
  • Findings support potential for late-season harvest management
doi:10.1111/nph.70313
  • Evidence for local adaptation in switchgrass-rust pathogen interactions
doi:10.1111/gcbb.70051
  • Prior agricultural management practices have lasting effects on switchgrass establishment and growth
pmid:40693769 Using DNA affinity purification sequencing (DAP-seq) to identify in vitro binding sites of potential Novosphingobium aromaticivorans DSM12444 transcription factors.
  • DAP-seq was used to identify in vitro binding sites of potential transcription factors in Novosphingobium aromaticivorans DSM12444.
  • The linked BioProject describes the study objective as identifying putative transcriptional regulators of aromatic metabolism in Novosphingobium aromaticivorans.
doi:10.1039/C8GC03504K Funneling aromatic products of chemically depolymerized lignin into 2-pyrone-4,6-dicarboxylic acid with Novosphingobium aromaticivorans
  • Engineered N. aromaticivorans DSM12444 funnels heterogeneous lignin-derived aromatics into a single product, 2-pyrone-4,6-dicarboxylic acid (PDC), a precursor for polyesters and epoxy adhesives
  • Biological funneling exploits converging catabolic pathways in N. aromaticivorans that simultaneously metabolize G, S, and H lignin-derived monomers and oligomers
doi:10.1128/mbio.01718-24 Catabolism of beta-5 linked aromatics by Novosphingobium aromaticivorans
  • N. aromaticivorans catabolizes beta-5 linked lignin dimers, expanding the range of lignin depolymerization products that can be funneled into valuable chemicals
doi:10.1039/D4GC01975J Achieving high productivity of 2-pyrone-4,6-dicarboxylic acid from aqueous aromatic streams with Novosphingobium aromaticivorans
  • Demonstrated high-productivity PDC production from aqueous aromatic streams, advancing N. aromaticivorans toward industrial-scale lignin valorization
doi:10.1128/aem.02081-24 Laboratory evolution in Novosphingobium aromaticivorans enables rapid catabolism of a model lignin-derived aromatic dimer
  • Adaptive laboratory evolution generated N. aromaticivorans strains with improved catabolism of guaiacylglycerol-beta-guaiacyl ether (GGE), a model lignin dimer with a beta-O-4 linkage
  • Identified causal mutation in an uncharacterized gene that doubled growth yield on GGE, with the encoded enzyme oxidizing beta-hydroxypropiovanillone to vanilloyl acetaldehyde
doi:10.1128/msystems.00187-22 iNovo479: Metabolic Modeling Provides a Roadmap to Optimize Bioproduct Yield from Deconstructed Lignin Aromatics by Novosphingobium aromaticivorans
  • Genome-scale metabolic model iNovo479 predicts product yields from single or multiple lignin-derived aromatics and guides engineering of N. aromaticivorans for lignin valorization
doi:10.1128/aem.01268-23 Production of carotenoids from aromatics and pretreated lignocellulosic biomass by Novosphingobium aromaticivorans
  • Engineered N. aromaticivorans produces carotenoids (beta-carotene, zeaxanthin, astaxanthin) concurrently with PDC from alkaline pretreated lignocellulosic biomass
Datasets 2
Switchgrass Reference Genome

High-quality reference genome assembly for switchgrass (Panicum virgatum), the preeminent American perennial C4 bioenergy crop for cellulosic ethanol. Genome assembly based on 732 resequenced genotypes grown across 10 common gardens spanning 1,800 km of latitude.

Repository: JGI Phytozome
URL: https://phytozome.jgi.doe.gov/
Data type: genomics
Reference: doi:10.1038/s41586-020-03127-1 - Genomic mechanisms of climate adaptation in polyploid bioenergy switchgrass
KBS Lux Arbor Reserve Switchgrass Flux Data

Eddy covariance flux tower data from switchgrass plots at the Lux Arbor Reserve. Measures carbon, water, and energy exchanges between switchgrass ecosystem and atmosphere.

Repository: Ameriflux
DOI: 10.17190/AMF/1644212
Data type: environmental sensor data
Dataset Variables 3
Variable Roles Value Context Mappings
net ecosystem carbon dioxide exchange
Ecosystem-atmosphere carbon dioxide exchange measured by eddy covariance.
Measured Variable Study Design Dependent Variable
Numeric
Units umol CO2 m-2 s-1
Entity switchgrass ecosystem-atmosphere carbon exchange
Method eddy covariance flux tower
Time series yes; sub-daily tower observations
Fields NEE, FC
BERVO total canopy net CO2 exchange BERVO:0000326
total evaporation
Water loss from the ecosystem inferred from flux tower observations.
Measured Variable Study Design Dependent Variable
Numeric
Units mm
Entity switchgrass ecosystem water exchange
Method eddy covariance flux tower
Time series yes; sub-daily tower observations
Fields ET, LE
BERVO total evaporation BERVO:0000315
soil temperature
Soil temperature recorded as an environmental covariate for interpreting fluxes.
Measured Variable Covariate
Numeric
Units degree Celsius
Entity soil environment
Matrix soil
Method flux tower meteorological sensor
Time series yes; sub-daily tower observations
Fields TS, soil_temperature
BERVO soil temperature BERVO:0001477
MIxS soil temperature MIXS:0001163
Outputs: Publications: >1800 peer-reviewed papers | Patent applications: 286 | Startups: 5
JBEI Joint BioEnergy Institute

JBEI is dedicated to developing advanced biofuels—liquid fuels derived from solar energy stored in plant biomass—that can replace gasoline, diesel, and jet fuels. JBEI takes an interdisciplinary approach combining expertise in synthetic biology, plant biology, and chemical engineering.

Lead institution: Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory
Partners:
Sandia National Laboratories Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory University Of California, Berkeley University Of California, Davis Iowa State University Carnegie Institution For Science
Location: Emeryville, California
Funding: DOE BER / Genomic Science Program (2023-2028)
Start date: 2007
Established: 2007
Principal investigators:
Jay D. Keasling
CEO
Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory / UC Berkeley
Blake A. Simmons
Chief Science and Technology Officer
Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory
Henrik Vibe Scheller
VP of Feedstocks
Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory
Links:
Main Doe Page Github
NMDC umbrella study: nmdc:sty-11-ggfd7z76
Scientific questions:
  • How can we engineer plants for improved biomass production?
  • What ionic liquid pretreatments optimize biomass deconstruction?
  • How do we engineer microbes for efficient biofuel production?
  • What metabolic pathways can produce drop-in biofuels?
  • How can synthetic biology accelerate biofuel development?
Keywords:
Biofuels Synthetic Biology Ionic Liquids Metabolic Engineering Plant Cell Walls Lignin Valorization Terpenes Fatty Acids Microbial Chassis
Data types:
Genomics Transcriptomics Metabolomics Proteomics Synthetic Biology Constructs Enzyme Kinetics
Modalities:
High Throughput Screening Robotic Automation Mass Spectrometry Nmr Spectroscopy Computational Modeling
Studies 2
nmdc:sty-11-n7mtj961 - Impact of modulating bioenergy traits on the sorghum microbiome under drought conditions (Henrik Vibe Scheller)

16S amplicon sequences from the rhizosphere of sorghum under well-watered and drought-stressed conditions in Davis, California.

BioProjects: PRJNA1205755
Source: Impact of modulating bioenergy traits on the sorghum microbiome under drought conditions
NCBI BioProject record for JBEI sorghum microbiome amplicon sequencing across soil, rhizosphere, and root endosphere samples.

Sorghum Synthetic Rhizosphere Community (SRC1) (Devin Coleman-Derr)

Defined synthetic microbial community (SRC1) of 16-20 bacterial strains assembled from sorghum rhizosphere isolates to enhance bioenergy crop productivity. Field-tested with increased shoot biomass correlating with transcriptional dampening of lignin biosynthesis.

Organism: Sorghum bicolor
Data modalities:
Amplicon 16s Metagenomics
Primary reference: doi:10.1093/ismejo/wrae126 - Defined synthetic microbial communities colonize and benefit field-grown sorghum
Study Design
Design type: study design OBI:0500000
Experimental units: field-grown sorghum plants
Observational units: sorghum rhizosphere samples, sorghum shoot phenotype measurements
Sampling strategy: Field test of defined sorghum rhizosphere synthetic community colonization and plant performance effects.
Controls: Non-inoculated sorghum plants used as the baseline for synthetic community effects.
Notes: Variable catalog entry based on study description and primary publication.
Study Variables 4
Variable Roles Value Context Mappings
SRC1 inoculation
Defined sorghum rhizosphere synthetic community treatment applied to field-grown plants.
Experimental Factor Study Design Independent Variable
Categorical
Levels non-inoculated control, SRC1 inoculated
Entity sorghum rhizosphere inoculation treatment
Fields inoculation_treatment, syncom_treatment, SRC1
MIxS perturbation MIXS:0000754
MIxS experimental factor MIXS:0000008
synthetic community strain abundance
Strain or member abundance of SRC1 bacteria after field colonization.
Study Design Dependent Variable Measured Variable
Array
Entity SRC1 bacterial community
Matrix sorghum rhizosphere
Method amplicon sequencing and metagenomics
Fields strain_abundance, syncom_member_abundance, community_profile
MIxS environmental medium MIXS:0000014
shoot biomass
Sorghum shoot biomass response associated with SRC1 colonization.
Study Design Dependent Variable Measured Variable
Numeric
Entity sorghum shoot
Method field biomass phenotyping
Fields shoot_biomass, aboveground_biomass, biomass
lignin biosynthesis transcript abundance
Host transcriptional response for lignin biosynthesis associated with SRC1 field colonization.
Study Design Dependent Variable Measured Variable
Array
Entity sorghum lignin biosynthesis genes
Method plant transcript profiling
Fields lignin_biosynthesis_expression, transcript_abundance, gene_expression_profile
Key Publications 10
doi:10.1038/nature04607 Designed divergent evolution of enzyme function
doi:10.1038/s41586-019-0978-9 Complete biosynthesis of cannabinoids and their unnatural analogues in yeast
doi:10.1038/s41467-020-18008-4 A machine learning Automated Recommendation Tool for synthetic biology
  • ART enables active learning-guided optimization of metabolic engineering, successfully applied to design pathways, choose downregulation targets, and optimize media
doi:10.21203/rs.3.rs-6846062/v1 Automation and machine learning drive rapid optimization of isoprenol production in Pseudomonas putida
  • Integration of laboratory automation with machine learning (ART) reduced DBTL cycle turnaround from months to two weeks, achieving 5-fold titer improvement in isoprenol production
doi:10.1038/s42003-025-08039-2 Machine learning-led semi-automated medium optimization reveals salt as key for flaviolin production in Pseudomonas putida
  • Active learning approach identified NaCl as critical medium component for flaviolin production, demonstrating non-intuitive solutions from AI-guided optimization
doi:10.3390/bioengineering12080788 High-Throughput Microfluidic Electroporation (HTME): A Scalable, 384-Well Platform for Multiplexed Cell Engineering
  • HTME platform electroporates 384 wells in under one minute with individual well control, enabling high-throughput strain construction for ML-guided metabolic engineering
doi:10.1126/sciadv.adj0053 Lignin conversion to beta-ketoadipic acid by Pseudomonas putida via metabolic engineering and bioprocess development
  • Engineered P. putida KT2440 converts mixed lignin-derived aromatics to beta-ketoadipic acid, a precursor for performance-advantaged nylons, at titers of 44.5 g/L from model compounds and 25 g/L from corn stover-derived streams
  • Technoeconomic analysis predicts a minimum selling price of $2.01/kg for beta-ketoadipate, cost-competitive with fossil-derived adipic acid
doi:10.1016/j.meteng.2021.02.004 Metabolism of syringyl lignin-derived compounds in Pseudomonas putida enables convergent production of 2-pyrone-4,6-dicarboxylic acid
  • Engineered P. putida converges S-lignin-derived aromatics into 2-pyrone-4,6-dicarboxylic acid (PDC), expanding biological funneling beyond G-lignin substrates
doi:10.1039/d0gc01663b Generation of ionic liquid tolerant Pseudomonas putida KT2440 strains via adaptive laboratory evolution
  • Adaptive laboratory evolution generated P. putida strains tolerant to ionic liquids used in biomass pretreatment, enabling one-pot bioprocessing of IL-treated lignocellulose
  • Evolved strains grow at IL concentrations (4% TEOH-OAc, 8% TEA-HS) lethal to wild-type, with mutations in efflux pump emrE and glyoxylate cycle regulator PP_5350
doi:10.1021/acssuschemeng.1c00933 Muconic Acid Production Using Engineered Pseudomonas putida KT2440 and a Guaiacol-Rich Fraction Derived from Kraft Lignin
  • Engineered P. putida produces cis,cis-muconic acid from guaiacol derived from kraft lignin depolymerization via O-demethylation to catechol followed by intradiol ring cleavage
Datasets 10
JBEI ICE (Inventory of Composable Elements)

Registry of standard biological parts for synthetic biology

URL: https://github.com/jbei
Data type: synthetic biology parts
ART (Automated Recommendation Tool)

Machine learning tool for active learning-guided optimization of metabolic engineering. Successfully used for pathway design, downregulation target selection, media optimization, and CRISPR guide RNA design.

URL: https://github.com/JBEI/ART
Data type: computational tool
bioPKS

Computational tool for recommending pathways to synthesize desired molecules via retrobiosynthesis

Data type: computational tool
ClusterCAD

Updated computational platform for chimeric type I polyketide synthase and nonribosomal peptide synthetase design

URL: https://clustercad.jbei.org
Data type: computational tool
Macaw

Accessible tool for molecular embedding and inverse molecular design, predicting properties of prospective molecular targets

Data type: computational tool
GenSLM

Genome-scale language model for DNA sequence analysis and generation, used for synthetic biology part design

Data type: computational tool
OpenFold3-PPI

Next-generation protein-protein interaction model under development with Columbia University for de novo enzyme design

Data type: computational tool
HTME (High-Throughput Microfluidic Electroporation)

Microfluidic platform capable of electroporating 384 wells in under one minute with individual control of each well. Eliminates key bottleneck for high-throughput strain construction enabling ML-guided metabolic engineering.

Data type: automation infrastructure
Echo 550 Acoustic Liquid Handler

Acoustic liquid handler for precise, low-volume liquid transfers without tips. Enables accurate dispensing for high-throughput strain construction and media optimization.

Data type: automation infrastructure
BioLector Cultivation System

Microbioreactor system for highly repeatable culture conditions ensuring generation of high-quality data for machine learning. Supports parallel cultivation with real-time monitoring.

Data type: automation infrastructure
Outputs: Publications: 685 (first 10 years) | Patent applications: 169 | Patents: 28 | Startups: 6
CABBI Center for Advanced Bioenergy and Bioproducts Innovation

CABBI develops fuels and products by integrating feedstock development, conversion science, and sustainability research. Using a "plants as factories" approach, CABBI researchers engineer crops to accumulate lipids and specialty chemicals while developing novel conversion technologies.

Lead institution: University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign
Partners:
Princeton University University Of California, Berkeley University Of Florida Iowa State University Colorado State University Hudsonalpha Institute For Biotechnology Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory Brookhaven National Laboratory
Location: Urbana, Illinois
Funding: DOE BER / Genomic Science Program (2023-2028)
Start date: 2017
Established: 2017
Principal investigators:
Evan H. DeLucia
Director Emeritus
University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign
Andrew Leakey
Director
University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign
Links:
Main Doe Page Github Data Hub
NMDC umbrella study: nmdc:sty-11-3cmn1g53
Scientific questions:
  • How can we engineer crops to produce oils and specialty chemicals?
  • What are sustainable approaches to bioenergy crop production?
  • How do we optimize lipid accumulation in vegetative tissues?
  • What conversion technologies maximize bioproduct yields?
  • How do land use changes affect carbon and ecosystem services?
Keywords:
Lipid Accumulation Sugarcane Miscanthus Sorghum Bioproducts Sustainability Life Cycle Assessment Oil Crops Metabolic Engineering
Data types:
Genomics Transcriptomics Metabolomics Field Trial Data Life Cycle Assessment Data Economic Modeling Data
Modalities:
Field Experiments Greenhouse Studies Metabolic Flux Analysis Techno Economic Analysis Ecosystem Modeling
Studies 6
HIGH nmdc:sty-11-vh2hty57 - The Impact of Stand Age and Fertilization on the Soil Microbiome of Miscanthus × giganteus (Adina Howe)

Investigation of effects of stand age and N fertilizer rates on microbial community structure in Miscanthus × giganteus replicated chronosequence trial.

Organism: Miscanthus × giganteus
Data modalities:
Amplicon 16s
Samples: 648
BioProjects: PRJNA601860
Primary reference: doi:10.1094/PBIOMES-01-20-0014-FI - The Impact of Stand Age and Fertilization on the Soil Microbiome of <i>Miscanthus × giganteus</i>
Study Design
Design type: study design OBI:0500000
Experimental units: Miscanthus chronosequence plots
Observational units: soil microbiome samples
Sampling strategy: Replicated chronosequence field sampling across stand ages and nitrogen fertilizer rates.
Replication: Replicated Miscanthus field plots across stand age and fertilizer-rate combinations.

HIGH Native Miscanthus Rhizosphere and Endophytic Microbiome (Angela Kent)

Survey of the native Miscanthus rhizosphere and endophytic microbiome in Taiwan, with particular attention to the diazotrophic community across strong biogeographical and ecological gradients in Miscanthus sinensis and M. floridulus.

Organism: Miscanthus sinensis, Miscanthus floridulus
Data modalities:
Amplicon 16s
Samples: 466
BioProjects: PRJNA561781
Primary reference: doi:10.1186/s40168-023-01646-3 - Host genetic variation drives the differentiation in the ecological role of the native Miscanthus root-associated microbiome
Source: Soil Metagenome - Native Miscanthus Rhizosphere and Endophytic Microbiome
CABBI dataset page linking the native Miscanthus microbiome BioProject and related publication.
Study Design
Design type: study design OBI:0500000
Observational units: rhizosphere samples, root endophytic compartment samples
Sampling strategy: Survey across native Miscanthus species, host genetic variation, sites, and ecological gradients in Taiwan.
Notes: Variable catalog entry based on CABBI dataset page and primary publication summary.

MEDIUM Soil Microbiome of Miscanthus × giganteus at LAMPS Study Site (Adina Howe)

SSU rRNA RNA library of soil microbiome at LAMPS. Complements DNA-based study examining total vs metabolically active microbial communities under nitrogen fertilization.

Organism: Miscanthus × giganteus
Data modalities:
Amplicon 16s
Samples: 271
BioProjects: PRJNA745191
Source: Soil microbiome of Miscanthus x giganteus at LAMPS Study Site

Observational units: soil microbiome samples
Sampling strategy: Soil sampling from LAMPS plots under nitrogen fertilization treatments.

HIGH Sorghum Rhizosphere Nitrification Study (Wendy Yang)

Intra- and inter-annual variability of nitrification in the rhizosphere of field-grown bioenergy sorghum, examining biological nitrification inhibition (BNI) potential and soil microbial communities across fertilizer treatments.

Organism: Sorghum bicolor
Data modalities:
Amplicon 16s
Samples: 288
BioProjects: PRJNA741261
Primary reference: doi:10.1111/gcbb.12917 - Intra- and inter-annual variability of nitrification in the rhizosphere of field-grown bioenergy sorghum
Source: Data for Intra- and inter-annual variability of nitrification in the rhizosphere of field-grown bioenergy sorghum
Illinois Data Bank dataset record documenting the nitrification measurements, soil chemistry, and PRJNA741261 microbial sequence data.
Study Design
Design type: study design OBI:0500000
Experimental units: field-grown sorghum plots
Observational units: sorghum rhizosphere soil samples
Sampling strategy: Repeated field sampling across years and fertilizer treatments to evaluate rhizosphere nitrification and microbial community variation.
Notes: Variable catalog entry based on study and publication descriptions.

Variable Roles Value Context Mappings
fertilizer treatment
Fertilizer treatment condition applied to field-grown bioenergy sorghum.
Experimental Factor Study Design Independent Variable
Categorical

Fields fertilizer_treatment, n_treatment, fertilizer_regimen
BERVO total fertilizer N amendment BERVO:0000895

MIxS experimental factor MIXS:0000008
sampling year
Field season used to evaluate inter-annual variability in rhizosphere nitrification.
Date Time Variable Study Design Independent Variable Grouping
Integer
Entity sampling event
Time series yes; annual and within-season sampling
Fields year, sampling_year, collection_date
MIxS collection date MIXS:0000011
nitrification activity
Rhizosphere nitrification response used to evaluate biological nitrification inhibition potential.
Study Design Dependent Variable Measured Variable
Numeric
Entity nitrification process
Matrix rhizosphere soil
Method soil nitrogen transformation assay
Fields nitrification_rate, potential_nitrification_rate, bni_activity
Notes BERVO mapping is to inhibition activity because the study frames the assay as biological nitrification inhibition potential; generic nitrification rate did not have a closer BERVO label.
BERVO current nitrification inhibition activity BERVO:0000871
rhizosphere microbial community composition
Amplicon-derived microbial community profile for sorghum rhizosphere samples.

Array

Matrix rhizosphere soil
Method 16S rRNA amplicon sequencing
Fields asv_table, otu_table, bacterial_community_profile
MIxS environmental medium MIXS:0000014

Investigation of soil microorganisms along 1 meter soil profiles in Miscanthus × giganteus, examining distinct mechanisms driving plant-nitrifier interactions at different depths.

Data modalities:
Amplicon 16s
Samples: 103
BioProjects: PRJNA1080623
Primary reference: doi:10.1016/j.soilbio.2024.109370 - Distinct mechanisms drive plant-nitrifier interactions in topsoil and subsoil

Observational units: topsoil microbiome samples, subsoil microbiome samples
Sampling strategy: One-meter soil profile sampling in Miscanthus fields to compare plant-nitrifier interactions across depth.

Categorical

Key Publications 1
doi:10.1094/PBIOMES-05-21-0033-MR Frontiers and Opportunities in Bioenergy Crop Microbiome Research Networks
Datasets 1
BEPAM Model Code and CABBI Simulation Results

Biofuel and Environmental Policy Analysis Model for assessing returns to land and greenhouse gas savings from energy crops.

Repository: Illinois Data Bank
DOI: 10.13012/B2IDB-5598609_V1
Data type: economic modeling
CBI Center for Bioenergy Innovation

CBI focuses on accelerating the domestication of bioenergy-relevant non-model plants and microbes to enable high-impact innovation across the sustainable aviation fuel supply chain. The center develops high-yielding, process-advantaged feedstock plants using advanced genetic technologies.

Lead institution: Oak Ridge National Laboratory
Partners:
University Of Tennessee Georgia Institute Of Technology National Renewable Energy Laboratory Dartmouth College University Of Georgia
Location: Oak Ridge, Tennessee
Funding: DOE BER / Genomic Science Program (2023-2028)
Start date: 2017
Established: 2017
Predecessor: BioEnergy Science Center (BESC, 2007-2017)
Principal investigators:
Gerald A. Tuskan
Director
Oak Ridge National Laboratory
Links:
Main Ornl Page Doe Page Publications Highlights
NMDC umbrella study: nmdc:sty-11-cssvjy19
Scientific questions:
  • How do we accelerate domestication of perennial bioenergy crops?
  • What genetic factors control biomass recalcitrance?
  • How can we engineer microbes for efficient biomass conversion?
  • What processes enable sustainable aviation fuel production?
  • How do plant-microbe interactions affect feedstock quality?
Keywords:
Poplar Switchgrass Sustainable Aviation Fuel Lignin Lignin Valorization Biomass Recalcitrance Genomic Selection Perennial Crops Populus Microbial Conversion
Data types:
Genomics Transcriptomics Metabolomics Proteomics Phenotypic Data Gwas Data
Modalities:
Genome Wide Association Field Trials Common Garden Experiments High Throughput Phenotyping Fermentation Studies
Studies 3
HIGH nmdc:sty-11-kfvmk798 - Chronic drought differentially alters the belowground microbiome of Populus trichocarpa (Melissa Cregger)

Assessment of archaeal/bacterial and fungal communities within root endosphere, rhizosphere, and bulk soil of P. trichocarpa genotypes under drought in Boardman, OR.

Organism: Populus trichocarpa
Data modalities:
Amplicon 16s Amplicon Its
Samples: 384
BioProjects: PRJNA784967
Primary reference: doi:10.1094/PBIOMES-12-21-0076-R - Chronic Drought Differentially Alters the Belowground Microbiome of Drought-Tolerant and Drought-Susceptible Genotypes of <i>Populus trichocarpa</i>
Study Design
Design type: study design OBI:0500000
Experimental units: Populus trichocarpa genotypes
Observational units: root endosphere microbiome samples, rhizosphere microbiome samples, bulk soil microbiome samples
Sampling strategy: Boardman field sampling across Populus genotypes, belowground compartments, and drought conditions.
Notes: Variable catalog entry based on CBI study description.

HIGH Poplar Rhizosphere Microbiota Assembly Dynamics (Aurelie Deveau)

Characterization of fungal and bacterial community assembly from root to shoot axis in poplar (P. tremula x tremuloides) over 30 days of growth, tracking changes in rhizosphere, root and shoot by DNA metabarcoding alongside metabolomics.

Organism: Populus tremula x tremuloides
Data modalities:
Amplicon 16s Amplicon Its Metabolomics
Samples: 505
BioProjects: PRJNA1017804
Source: Mechanisms of regulation of the rhizosphere, roots and shoots of naive poplars

MEDIUM Clostridium thermocellum Strain Collection (Daniel Olson)

Whole genome resequencing data for hundreds of Clostridium thermocellum strains engineered for increased ethanol production and tolerance, with mutation and lineage metadata.

Source: Whole genome resequencing data from a collection of Clostridium Thermocellum strains
CBI published data page for the Clostridium thermocellum strain resequencing dataset.
Study Design
Design type: study design OBI:0500000
Experimental units: engineered Clostridium thermocellum strains
Observational units: resequenced strain genomes
Sampling strategy: Whole-genome resequencing of engineered C. thermocellum lineages selected for ethanol production and tolerance phenotypes.
Notes: Variable catalog entry based on CBI study description.

Array

Fields mutation, variant, genotype
ethanol production phenotype
Ethanol production level associated with engineered or evolved strains.

Fields ethanol_titer, ethanol_yield, production_phenotype
ethanol tolerance phenotype
Ethanol tolerance or growth response phenotype associated with strain lineage.
Study Design Dependent Variable Measured Variable
Numeric
Entity C. thermocellum growth tolerance
Method ethanol tolerance assay
Fields ethanol_tolerance, growth_rate, tolerance_phenotype
Key Publications 4
doi:10.1002/bbb.2148
doi:10.1038/s41467-019-11376-6 Quantitative fermentation of unpretreated transgenic poplar by Caldicellulosiruptor bescii
  • Metabolically engineered C. bescii directly ferments transgenic low-lignin poplar without pretreatment, demonstrating consolidated bioprocessing of woody biomass
  • C. bescii with C. thermocellum adhE gene produces ethanol directly from unpretreated plant biomass, bypassing costly pretreatment steps for lignin-rich feedstocks
doi:10.1016/j.biortech.2023.129189 Rewiring metabolism of Clostridium thermocellum for consolidated bioprocessing of lignocellulosic biomass poplar to produce short-chain esters
  • Engineered C. thermocellum produces short-chain esters directly from poplar biomass via consolidated bioprocessing, with up to 80-fold improvement through carbohydrate esterase deletion and alcohol acetyltransferase overexpression
doi:10.1038/s41563-024-02024-6 Lignin deoxygenation for the production of sustainable aviation fuel blendstocks
  • Continuous two-stage catalytic process using molybdenum carbide deoxygenates poplar lignin into jet-range aromatic hydrocarbons with 87.5% selectivity and 86% carbon recovery
  • Lignin-derived SAF aromatics are performance-advantaged relative to conventional jet fuel aromatics and lignin can meet current aromatic demand 2.5 times over
Datasets 1
Populus GWAS Dataset

Over 28 million SNPs from approximately 882 resequenced poplar genotypes, linking DNA variations to plant characteristics.

Data type: genomics/GWAS
Size: ~17 terabytes raw data
Genomic Science SFAs (13)
ENIGMA ENIGMA: Ecosystems and Networks Integrated with Genes and Molecular Assemblies

ENIGMA emphasizes achieving a multiscale, causal, and predictive understanding of microbial biology and the reciprocal impact of microbial communities on their ecosystems. Focus on processes affecting denitrification and metal reduction in contaminated subsurface environments.

Lead institution: Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory
Funding: DOE BER / Genomic Science Program
Principal investigators:
Paul D. Adams
Project Manager
Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory
Adam P. Arkin
Technical Co-manager
Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory
Links:
Main Doe Page Kbase
KBase Collection: ENIGMA
Scientific questions:
  • What mechanisms drive subcellular, cellular, and intercellular networks?
  • How do microbial networks drive macroscopic biogeochemical processes?
  • What controls denitrification and metal reduction in subsurface?
  • How do microbial communities respond to contamination?
Keywords:
Microbial Ecosystems Denitrification Metal Reduction Uranium Nitrate Subsurface Microbiology Systems Biology Metagenomics
Data types:
Metagenomics Transcriptomics Proteomics Metabolomics Structural Biology Environmental Sensor Data
Modalities:
High Throughput Sequencing Mass Spectrometry Imaging Synchrotron Methods Computational Modeling
Phenotype assays:
Rbtseq Fitness Stress Tolerance Anaerobic Growth Substrate Utilization Growth Curves Coculture Interactions
Field Sites 1
Oak Ridge Reservation (ORR) (East Tennessee)

Bear Creek aquifer with complex contaminant gradients

Contaminants:
Nitrates Uranium Volatile Organic Carbon Species
Programs: ENIGMA: Ecosystems and Networks Integrated with Genes and Molecular Assemblies
Isolate Collections 1
ENIGMA Subsurface Isolates 261 isolates

Bacterial and archaeal isolates from Oak Ridge Reservation subsurface environments including contaminated groundwater and sediments. Characterized for stress tolerance and denitrification/metal reduction capabilities.

Organism types:
Bacteria Archaea
Genomes sequenced: 261
Source environments:
Oak Ridge Subsurface Contaminated Groundwater Aquifer Sediments
Isolation methods: Aerobic plating, Anaerobic enrichment
Genome catalog: View genomes
Reference: doi:10.1128/msystems.00537-21 - Ecogenomics of Groundwater Phages Suggests Niche Differentiation Linked to Specific Environmental Tolerance
Studies 4
HIGH Oak Ridge Groundwater 100-Well Survey Metagenomes (Jizhong Zhou)

Comprehensive metagenomic survey of groundwater microbial communities from 100 wells at the Oak Ridge Field Research Center. Examines community assembly processes under environmental stress from contamination gradients.

Organism: groundwater metagenome
Data modalities:
Metagenomics
BioProjects: PRJNA1001011
Primary reference: doi:10.1038/s41564-023-01573-x - Environmental stress mediates groundwater microbial community assembly
Study Design
Design type: study design OBI:0500000
Observational units: groundwater well metagenome samples
Sampling strategy: Metagenomic survey across 100 Oak Ridge Field Research Center groundwater wells spanning environmental stress and contamination gradients.
Replication: 100 groundwater wells.
Notes: Variable catalog entry based on study description and primary publication summary.

Fields metagenome_profile, taxonomic_profile, functional_profile

community assembly process estimate

Study Design Dependent Variable Derived
Numeric

Fields stochastic_assembly_fraction, selection_process_estimate, assembly_process

26 subsurface sediment and 9 groundwater metagenomes from the Oak Ridge Reservation studying subsurface microbiome from centimeter to field scale.

MEDIUM ENIGMA Groundwater Archaeal Diversity (Jillian F. Banfield)

Genome-resolved metagenomic analysis of archaeal diversity in subsurface environments. Identified two new phyla (DPANN superphylum) from aquifer adjacent to Colorado River.

Organism: groundwater metagenome
Data modalities:
Metagenomics
BioProjects: PRJNA268032
Primary reference: doi:10.1016/j.cub.2015.01.014 - Genomic expansion of domain archaea highlights roles for organisms from new phyla in anaerobic carbon cycling
Study Design
Design type: study design OBI:0500000
Observational units: aquifer metagenome samples, archaeal genome bins
Sampling strategy: Genome-resolved metagenomic analysis of groundwater and aquifer samples to recover archaeal diversity.
Notes: Variable catalog entry based on study description.

Fields archaeal_mag_id, genome_bin, bin_id
archaeal taxonomic assignment
Taxonomic placement of recovered archaeal genomes, including DPANN superphylum phyla.
Measured Variable Derived
Ontology Term
Entity archaeal genome
Method genome-resolved taxonomic classification
Fields taxonomy, archaeal_phylum, DPANN_assignment
archaeal diversity profile
Genome-resolved diversity profile of archaeal lineages in groundwater subsurface samples.
Study Design Dependent Variable Measured Variable
Array
Entity groundwater archaeal community
Matrix groundwater
Method metagenomic sequencing and genome-resolved analysis
Fields archaeal_diversity, archaeal_profile, genome_resolved_profile
Denitrifying Synthetic Community (Adam P. Arkin)

Two-member denitrifying synthetic community of Rhodanobacter thiooxydans FW510-R12 and Acidovorax sp. GW101-3H11 from the Oak Ridge contaminated subsurface. Exhibits pathway-partitioned denitrification via nitrite and nitric oxide exchange.

Organism: groundwater isolates
Data modalities:
Genomics Transcriptomics Proteomics
Primary reference: doi:10.1093/ismejo/wraf093 - Emergence and disruption of cooperativity in a denitrifying microbial community
Source: The Use of Synthetic Communities Reveals Disturbance of Process Partitioning Among Denitrifying Microbes Leads to Increased Nitrous Oxide Emissions
DOE Genomic Science abstract describing the ENIGMA denitrifying SynCom and its process-partitioned denitrification experiments.
Study Design
Design type: study design OBI:0500000
Experimental units: denitrifying synthetic community cultures
Observational units: mono-culture and co-culture omics samples
Sampling strategy: Genomic, transcriptomic, and proteomic profiling of a two-member Oak Ridge denitrifying synthetic community.
Controls: Single-member cultures used to interpret two-member community interactions.
Notes: Variable catalog entry based on study description.
Study Variables 4
Variable Roles Value Context Mappings
synthetic community member
Rhodanobacter or Acidovorax member represented in the denitrifying synthetic community.
Study Design Independent Variable Grouping Identifier
Categorical
Levels Rhodanobacter thiooxydans FW510-R12, Acidovorax sp. GW101-3H11
Entity denitrifying bacterial strain
Fields strain, organism, community_member
culture configuration
Whether the sample represents a single-member culture or two-member synthetic community.
Experimental Factor Study Design Independent Variable
Categorical
Levels monoculture, co-culture
Entity denitrifying culture
Fields culture_configuration, coculture_condition, community_state
MIxS experimental factor MIXS:0000008
denitrification intermediate exchange
Nitrite and nitric oxide exchange associated with pathway-partitioned denitrification.
Study Design Dependent Variable Measured Variable
Array
Entity denitrification metabolites
Matrix culture medium
Method metabolite or pathway analysis
Fields nitrite_exchange, nitric_oxide_exchange, denitrification_intermediate
transcript and protein abundance profile
Expression and proteomic profiles used to characterize pathway partitioning in the denitrifying synthetic community.
Study Design Dependent Variable Measured Variable
Array
Entity denitrifying synthetic community
Matrix microbial culture
Method transcriptomics and proteomics
Fields transcript_abundance, protein_abundance, pathway_profile
Analyses & KBase Narratives 2
ENIGMA public isolates from Kothari et al 2020

261 ENIGMA isolate genomes from Oak Ridge subsurface with GTDB taxonomy assignments. Includes sequencing reads, assemblies, and annotations processed via KBase workflows.

Type: Kbase Narrative
Genomes: 261
Output types:
Genomics
Reference: doi:10.1128/msystems.00537-21 - Ecogenomics of Groundwater Phages Suggests Niche Differentiation Linked to Specific Environmental Tolerance
Data from Kothari et al 2020

Supporting data narrative for ENIGMA isolate phenotyping study

Type: Kbase Narrative
Key Publications 2
doi:10.1038/s41564-023-01573-x Environmental stress mediates groundwater microbial community assembly
  • Stochastic assembly processes are critical (>60% on average) in shaping groundwater community structure, but decrease in importance as environmental stress increases.
  • Selection processes leading to dissimilar communities increased with stresses, primarily related to pH, cobalt and molybdenum.
doi:10.2172/1963932
Datasets 1
ENIGMA Environmental Data

Multi-omics data from Oak Ridge Reservation subsurface

Repository: KBase
Data type: metagenomics/environmental
m-CAFEs m-CAFEs: Microbial Community Analysis and Functional Evaluation in Soils

m-CAFEs develops and deploys unique laboratory capabilities to capture key aspects of grass rhizosphere communities and identify causal mechanisms governing microbial interactions within the rhizosphere. Uses advanced fabricated ecosystems with gene editing technologies for interrogating gene and microbial functions in situ.

Lead institution: Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory
Partners:
University Of California, Berkeley North Carolina State University Boston University Sandia National Laboratories
Funding: DOE BER / Genomic Science Program
Principal investigators:
Trent R. Northen
Principal Investigator
Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory
Links:
Main Doe Page
NMDC umbrella study: nmdc:sty-11-abkzcd11
Scientific questions:
  • What mechanisms govern microbial interactions in the rhizosphere?
  • How can we predict and manipulate rhizosphere communities?
  • What controls carbon cycling in plant-microbe-soil systems?
  • How do microbiomes respond to perturbations?
Keywords:
Rhizosphere Soil Microbiome Fabricated Ecosystems Crispr Phage Carbon Cycling Brachypodium Ecofab
Data types:
Metagenomics Transcriptomics Metabolomics Exometabolomics Imaging Data
Modalities:
Fabricated Ecosystems High Throughput Sequencing Crispr Gene Editing Live Cell Imaging
Phenotype assays:
Ecofab Phenotyping Exometabolomics Synthetic Community Plant Colonization Substrate Utilization Growth Curves Live Cell Imaging
Isolate Collections 2
m-CAFEs Rhizosphere Bacterial Collection

Bacterial isolates from Brachypodium and grass rhizospheres used for synthetic community studies in EcoFAB devices. Includes reduced complexity consortia (RCC) strains representing core rhizosphere microbiome.

Organism types:
Bacteria
Source environments:
Brachypodium Rhizosphere Grass Rhizosphere Angelo Coast Range Reserve Soil
Host organisms: Brachypodium distachyon, Avena barbata
Isolation methods: Aerobic plating, Dilution-to-extinction
m-CAFEs Phage Collection

Bacteriophages isolated from soil for phage-based genome editing and microbial community manipulation studies.

Organism types:
Phage
Source environments:
Soil Rhizosphere
Studies 8
nmdc:sty-11-zs2syx06 - Meadow Soil Metagenomes from the Angelo Coast Range Reserve (Jillian F. Banfield)

Assembled metagenomes for 60 soil samples across multiple soil plots from a meadow in the Angelo Coastal Range Reserve, CA, part of a climate change experiment.

Primary reference: doi:10.1038/s41564-019-0449-y - Mediterranean grassland soil C-N compound turnover is dependent on rainfall and depth, and is mediated by genomically divergent microorganisms
Study Design
Design type: study design OBI:0500000
Observational units: meadow soil metagenome samples
Sampling strategy: Soil metagenome sampling across multiple plots in a meadow climate change experiment at the Angelo Coast Range Reserve.
Replication: 60 soil samples.
Notes: Variable catalog entry based on study description.

nmdc:sty-11-ev70y104 - EcoFAB 2.0 Root Microbiome Ring Trial (Trent R. Northen)

Multi-laboratory ring trial using EcoFAB 2.0 devices with synthetic bacterial communities and Brachypodium distachyon to test reproducibility and standardization.

Source: EcoFAB 2.0 Root Microbiome Ring Trial
MassIVE dataset DOI for the multi-laboratory EcoFAB 2.0 root microbiome ring trial metabolomics data.

Observational units: Brachypodium root microbiome samples, synthetic community abundance profiles

Replication: Replicated across participating laboratories.

TARGET Brachypodium Core Root Microbiome Metabarcoding (Trent R. Northen)

16S rRNA amplicon sequencing defining core root microbiome of Brachypodium distachyon grown in four distinct soils. Identified overlooked taxa via metabarcoding vs culture.

Organism: Brachypodium distachyon
Data modalities:
Amplicon 16s
Samples: 71
BioProjects: PRJNA1120948
Primary reference: doi:10.1007/s10725-024-01221-2 - Bacterial metabarcoding reveals plant growth promoting members of the core Brachypodium distachyon root-associated microbiome overlooked by culture dependent techniques
Study Design
Design type: study design OBI:0500000
Experimental units: Brachypodium plants
Observational units: root-associated microbiome samples
Sampling strategy: Bacterial metabarcoding of Brachypodium roots grown in four distinct soils, paired with culture-based isolation.
Notes: Variable catalog entry based on the primary publication summary and study description.
Study Variables 3
Variable Roles Value Context Mappings
soil origin
Source soil used to grow Brachypodium distachyon for core root microbiome analysis.
Study Design Independent Variable Grouping
Categorical
Levels four distinct soils
Entity plant growth soil
Fields soil_origin, soil_type, growth_soil
MIxS local environmental context MIXS:0000013
MIxS environmental medium MIXS:0000014
root-associated bacterial community composition
16S metabarcoding profile used to define the Brachypodium core root microbiome.
Study Design Dependent Variable Measured Variable
Array
Entity Brachypodium root-associated bacterial community
Matrix root-associated microbiome samples
Method 16S rRNA amplicon sequencing
Fields asv_table, otu_table, core_root_microbiome_profile
cultured isolate recovery
Culture-dependent recovery status or isolate set used to compare cultured and metabarcoded taxa.
Measured Variable Derived
Categorical
Entity root-associated bacterial taxon
Method bacterial isolation and culture comparison
Fields isolate_recovered, culture_status, isolate_id
TARGET Fine Scale Rhizosphere Spatial Heterogeneity (Trent R. Northen)

Fine scale sampling reveals spatial heterogeneity of rhizosphere microbiome in young Brachypodium plant roots using EcoFABs. Strong rhizosphere effect with Actinobacteria, Bacteroidetes, Firmicutes and Proteobacteria enrichment.

Organism: Brachypodium distachyon
Data modalities:
Amplicon 16s
Primary reference: doi:10.1038/s43705-023-00265-1 - Fine scale sampling reveals early differentiation of rhizosphere microbiome from bulk soil in young <i>Brachypodium</i> plant roots
Study Design
Design type: study design OBI:0500000
Experimental units: young Brachypodium plants
Observational units: root-zone microbiome samples
Sampling strategy: Fine-scale sampling of root tips, root bases, and bulk soil in EcoFAB and conventional growth containers.
Notes: Variable catalog entry based on the primary publication summary and study description.
Study Variables 4
Variable Roles Value Context Mappings
root zone
Fine-scale spatial zone along the developing primary root or bulk soil comparison region.
Study Design Independent Variable Spatial Index Grouping
Categorical
Levels root tip, root base, bulk soil
Entity Brachypodium root-zone habitat
Fields root_zone, spatial_zone, sample_position
MIxS local environmental context MIXS:0000013
MIxS environmental medium MIXS:0000014
growth container
Experimental growth system used for Brachypodium root microbiome sampling.
Experimental Factor Study Design Independent Variable Grouping
Categorical
Levels EcoFAB, pot, tube
Entity plant growth system
Fields container, growth_container, growth_system
MIxS plant growth medium MIXS:0001057
MIxS experimental factor MIXS:0000008
rhizosphere bacterial community composition
16S profile used to measure early differentiation of root-associated communities from bulk soil.
Study Design Dependent Variable Measured Variable
Array
Entity Brachypodium rhizosphere bacterial community
Matrix root-zone soil and bulk soil
Method 16S rRNA amplicon sequencing
Fields asv_table, otu_table, bacterial_community_profile
functional gene profile
Metagenomic functional profile contrasting root tips with bulk soil.
Study Design Dependent Variable Measured Variable
Array
Entity microbial functional potential
Matrix root tip and bulk soil
Method bulk metagenomics
Fields functional_profile, gene_family_abundance, pathway_abundance
TARGET Dynamic Root Exudate Chemistry and Microbial Assembly (Trent R. Northen)

Landmark study showing dynamic root exudate chemistry and microbial substrate preferences drive patterns in rhizosphere microbial community assembly. Avena barbata in natural soil.

Organism: Avena barbata
Data modalities:
Amplicon 16s Metabolomics Exometabolomics
Primary reference: doi:10.1038/s41564-018-0129-3 - Dynamic root exudate chemistry and microbial substrate preferences drive patterns in rhizosphere microbial community assembly
Study Design
Design type: time series design OBI:0500020
Experimental units: Avena barbata plants
Observational units: root exudate samples, rhizosphere microbiome samples
Sampling strategy: Time-resolved profiling of Avena root exudate chemistry, microbial substrate preferences, and rhizosphere community assembly in natural soil.
Notes: Variable catalog entry based on study description and primary publication.

Variable Roles Value Context Mappings
plant developmental time point

Categorical

Time series yes
Fields time_point, developmental_stage, collection_date

root exudate metabolite abundance

Array
Entity root exudate metabolome
Matrix root exudate
Method exometabolomics
Fields exudate_metabolite_abundance, metabolite_feature_table, exometabolome_profile

Array
Entity rhizosphere microorganisms
Method substrate utilization profiling linked to exudate chemistry
Fields substrate_preference, substrate_utilization, preferred_metabolite

Array

Matrix rhizosphere

Metabolomics Exometabolomics

TARGET Reduced Complexity Consortia from Brachypodium Rhizosphere (Trent R. Northen)

Systematic framework to generate Reduced Complexity Consortia (RCC) representing Brachypodium rhizosphere microbiome for sustainable agriculture applications. Inoculum from Angelo Coast Range Reserve soil.

Organism: Brachypodium distachyon
Data modalities:
Amplicon 16s Metagenomics
Primary reference: doi:10.3389/fmicb.2024.1401794 - Developing stable, simplified, functional consortia from Brachypodium rhizosphere for microbial application in sustainable agriculture
Study Design
Design type: study design OBI:0500000
Experimental units: reduced complexity consortium enrichment cultures
Observational units: Brachypodium rhizosphere community profiles, reduced consortium profiles
Sampling strategy: Systematic enrichment and profiling of reduced complexity consortia from Angelo Coast Range Reserve Brachypodium rhizosphere inoculum.
Notes: Variable catalog entry based on study description and primary publication.

Categorical
Entity consortium inoculum
Matrix rhizosphere soil
Fields inoculum_source, source_soil, source_rhizosphere

MIxS environmental medium MIXS:0000014
reduced consortium composition
Microbial composition of the reduced complexity consortium after enrichment.
Study Design Dependent Variable Measured Variable
Array
Entity reduced complexity microbial consortium

Fields consortium_composition, asv_table, metagenome_profile
consortium stability
Stability or reproducibility of consortium composition and function across transfers or replicates.
Study Design Dependent Variable Derived
Numeric
