Fields transformation_condition, protocol_condition, workflow_parameter
MIxS experimental factor MIXS:0000008
guide RNA target
CRISPRi guide RNA and target gene represented in high-throughput screens.
Study Design Independent Variable Identifier
Identifier
Entity CRISPRi perturbation
Fields guide_id, guide_sequence, target_gene
transformation efficiency
Efficiency of DNA transformation under tested workflow conditions.
Study Design Dependent Variable Measured Variable
Numeric
Entity bacterial transformation
Fields transformation_efficiency, colony_count, transformants_per_microgram
CRISPRi screen fitness effect
Growth or fitness effect measured for CRISPRi perturbations in non-model bacteria.
Study Design Dependent Variable Measured Variable Derived
Numeric
Entity bacterial CRISPRi perturbation
Method high-throughput CRISPRi screen
Fields fitness_effect, guide_enrichment, screen_score
Analyses & KBase Narratives 1
SEED Microbial Interaction Predictions

CommScores analysis for predicting pairwise interactions among SEED bacterial isolates including Bacillus velezensis. Uses same KBase apps as PMI SFA.

Type: Kbase Narrative
Output types:
Genomics
Key Publications 6
doi:10.1094/PHYTO-09-20-0433-A
  • B. velezensis EB14 contains gene clusters for antimicrobial compound biosynthesis
  • Produces iturin A, subtulene A, and fengycin cyclic lipopeptides
doi:10.1093/hr/uhae232
  • Identified plant-derived effector proteins regulating plant-fungal symbiosis
doi:10.1093/hr/uhad085
  • Developed CRISPR tools for gene activation and editing in Populus trees
doi:10.34133/2020/8051764 Plant Biosystems Design Research Roadmap 1.0
  • Established research roadmap for plant biosystems design and ecosystem engineering
doi:10.1093/plphys/kiad076 Expanding the Application of Anti-CRISPR Proteins in Plants for Tunable Genome Editing
  • Developed anti-CRISPR tools for tunable genome editing in plants
doi:10.1094/PHYTOFR-10-20-0023-R
  • B. velezensis EB14 shows strong antifungal activity against S. musiva
Datasets 1
Bacillus velezensis EB14 Complete Genome

Complete genome sequence of B. velezensis EB14 (4.0 Mb) sequenced using PacBio RS II. Contains predicted gene clusters for antimicrobial compound biosynthesis.

Repository: NCBI
URL: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/bioproject/PRJNA681597
BioSecure BioSecure: Building Robust Biocontainment Strategies for Evolutionarily Stable Microorganisms

The BioSecure SFA develops evolutionarily robust containment strategies for engineered environmental microorganisms, with emphasis on rhizosphere deployment. The program combines sequence entanglement, cellular controls, population-level coordination, high-throughput gene editing, and machine learning to keep engineered functions active only where and when they are intended.

Lead institution: Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory
Partners:
University Of California, Berkeley Columbia University University Of Pittsburgh
Funding: DOE BER / Genomic Science Program
Principal investigators:
Yongqin Jiao
Lead Principal Investigator
Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory
Co-investigators:
Dan Park
Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory
Mimi Yung
Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory
Dante Ricci
Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory
Jonathan Allen
Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory
Jennifer Listgarten
University of California, Berkeley
Harris Wang
Columbia University
W. Seth Childers
University of Pittsburgh
Links:
Doe Page Llnl Page
Scientific questions:
  • How do we deploy beneficial microbes safely in the rhizosphere?
  • What biocontainment mechanisms are evolutionarily stable?
  • How can machine learning improve sequence design for containment?
  • What population coordination strategies enhance containment?
  • How do we prevent potential negative ecological consequences of GEM persistence?
Keywords:
Plant Growth Promoting Bacteria Rhizosphere Biocontainment Shewanella Machine Learning Synthetic Biology Biosecurity Crispr
Data types:
Genomics Transcriptomics Proteomics Synthetic Biology Constructs Fitness Data Containment Efficacy Data
Studies 1
LOW Multilayered Biocontainment for Rhizosphere Deployment (Yongqin Jiao)

Developing and testing multilayered containment strategies for engineered microbes including sequence entanglement, kill-switches, environmental-cue controls, and population-level coordination.

Organism: engineered environmental microorganisms
Data modalities:
Genomics Transcriptomics
Source: BioSecure: Building Robust Biocontainment Strategies for Evolutionarily Stable Microorganisms
DOE Genomic Science program page describing the BioSecure SFA, personnel, research goals, and containment strategies.
Study Design
Design type: study design OBI:0500000
Experimental units: engineered Shewanella oneidensis strains, engineered Pseudomonas strains
Observational units: biocontainment construct records, containment assay measurements
Sampling strategy: Engineering and testing of multilayered kill-switch and sequence-entanglement containment designs for environmental microorganisms in rhizosphere-relevant contexts.
Notes: Variable catalog entry based on DOE and LLNL BioSecure SFA descriptions.
Study Variables 5
Variable Roles Value Context Mappings
engineered microbial strain
Engineered environmental microbial strain or chassis carrying a containment design.
Identifier Study Design Independent Variable
Identifier
Entity engineered environmental microorganism
Fields strain_id, chassis, engineered_strain
biocontainment design
Multilayered containment strategy including kill-switch or gene-overlap architecture.
Experimental Factor Study Design Independent Variable
Categorical
Levels sequence entanglement, kill-switch, unnatural amino acid dependence, chemical or light signaling control, quorum sensing coordination
Entity biocontainment circuit
Fields containment_design, kill_switch, sequence_entanglement
MIxS experimental factor MIXS:0000008
rhizosphere deployment context
Soil or rhizosphere environment used to test engineered microbe containment.
Study Design Independent Variable Grouping
Categorical
Entity deployment environment
Matrix rhizosphere
Fields deployment_context, environment, rhizosphere_context
MIxS local environmental context MIXS:0000013
MIxS environmental medium MIXS:0000014
Map rhizosphere ENVO:00005801
containment efficacy
Performance of the containment design in limiting persistence or escape of engineered microbes.
Study Design Dependent Variable Measured Variable
Numeric
Entity engineered microbial population
Method containment assay
Fields containment_efficacy, escape_frequency, survival_fraction
containment transcriptomic response
Gene expression response of engineered microbes carrying containment circuits.
Study Design Dependent Variable Measured Variable
Array
Entity engineered environmental microorganism
Method transcriptomics
Fields transcript_abundance, expression_profile, containment_response
Key Publications 2
doi:10.1093/nar/gkad484 Prolonging genetic circuit stability through adaptive evolution of overlapping genes
  • Overlapping-gene sequence entanglement can improve genetic circuit stability for biocontainment.
doi:10.1021/acssynbio.2c00386 Comparison of kill switch toxins in plant-beneficial Pseudomonas fluorescens reveals drivers of lethality, stability, and escape
  • Kill-switch performance in plant-beneficial Pseudomonas depends on toxin choice, stability, and escape mechanisms.
Datasets 1
BioSecure LLNL project page and research outputs

Program page describing BioSecure containment capabilities, publications, and team members.

Repository: LLNL Office of Science Programs
URL: https://sc-programs.llnl.gov/biological-and-environmental-research-at-llnl/secure-biosystems-design
Data type: program metadata and publication links
Environmental System Science SFAs (8)
Watershed Function Science Focus Area

The Watershed Function SFA develops predictive understanding of how mountainous watersheds retain and release water, nutrients, carbon, and metals. Research focuses on interacting impacts of warming and drought on hydro-biogeochemical functioning.

Lead institution: Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory
Funding: DOE BER / Environmental System Science (ESS)
Principal investigators:
Susan Hubbard
Lead Research Manager / ESD Director
Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory
Links:
Doe Page Ideas Watersheds Czen
NMDC umbrella study: nmdc:sty-11-2zhqs261
Scientific questions:
  • How do droughts and early snowmelt impact water availability?
  • What controls biogeochemical cycling in mountain watersheds?
  • How do hydrological, ecological, and biogeochemical processes interact?
  • What are the consequences of climate change for headwater systems?
Keywords:
Watershed Hydrology Biogeochemistry Upper Colorado River Basin Snowmelt Drought Mountain Hydrology Modex
Data types:
Hydrological Data Biogeochemical Data Remote Sensing Environmental Sensor Data Hydrological Data
Modalities:
Field Sensors Stream Sampling Groundwater Monitoring Remote Sensing Ecosystem Modeling
Phenotype assays:
Soil Incubation Respiration
Field Sites 2
East River Watershed (Colorado)

Mountainous community testbed in Upper Colorado River Basin, representative of vulnerable headwater systems

Programs: Watershed Function Science Focus Area, Colorado Headwaters Ecological Spectroscopy Study
Taylor River Watershed (Colorado)

Expansion site within Upper Colorado River Basin

Programs: Watershed Function Science Focus Area, Terrestrial Wetland Function and Resilience Science Focus Area, Colorado Headwaters Ecological Spectroscopy Study
Studies 2
HIGH East River Floodplain Metagenomes (Eoin Brodie)

Metagenome-assembled genomes from floodplain soils in the East River watershed. 248 draft quality genomes reconstructed at sub-species level from upper, middle, and lower reaches. Demonstrates floodplains as scaling motifs for watershed models.

Organism: soil metagenome
Data modalities:
Metagenomics
Primary reference: doi:10.1186/s40168-020-00957-z - Meanders as a scaling motif for understanding of floodplain soil microbiome and biogeochemical potential at the watershed scale
Study Design
Design type: study design OBI:0500000
Observational units: East River floodplain soil metagenome samples, metagenome-assembled genomes
Sampling strategy: Floodplain soil metagenomics across upper, middle, and lower reaches of the East River watershed.
Replication: 248 draft quality genomes reconstructed at sub-species level.
Notes: Variable catalog entry based on study description.
Study Variables 4
Variable Roles Value Context Mappings
floodplain reach
Watershed reach represented by each floodplain soil metagenome sample.
Study Design Independent Variable Spatial Index Grouping
Categorical
Levels upper reach, middle reach, lower reach
Entity East River floodplain
Matrix floodplain soil
Fields reach, watershed_position, floodplain_reach
MIxS local environmental context MIXS:0000013
floodplain soil metagenome profile
Taxonomic and functional metagenome profile for East River floodplain soils.
Study Design Dependent Variable Measured Variable
Array
Entity floodplain soil microbial community
Matrix floodplain soil
Method metagenomic sequencing
Fields metagenome_profile, taxonomic_profile, functional_profile
MIxS environmental medium MIXS:0000014
metagenome-assembled genome
Draft quality MAG reconstructed from East River floodplain soil metagenomes.
Derived Identifier
Identifier
Entity floodplain soil microbial genome
Method metagenome assembly and binning
Fields mag_id, genome_bin, genome_id
watershed model scaling motif
Derived motif or feature used to represent floodplains in watershed models.
Derived Study Design Dependent Variable
Array
Entity watershed model representation
Method genome-resolved metagenomic interpretation for watershed model scaling
Fields scaling_motif, floodplain_motif, model_feature
MEDIUM East River Virome and Microbial Dynamics (Simon Roux)

Virus diversity and activity driven by snowmelt and host dynamics in high-altitude watershed soil ecosystem. Part of the Watershed Function SFA microbiome studies.

Organism: soil metagenome
Data modalities:
Metagenomics Metatranscriptomics
Primary reference: doi:10.1186/s40168-023-01666-z - Virus diversity and activity is driven by snowmelt and host dynamics in a high-altitude watershed soil ecosystem
Study Design
Design type: study design OBI:0500000
Observational units: high-altitude watershed soil samples
Sampling strategy: Seasonal soil sampling across snowmelt-linked watershed conditions to compare viral diversity, viral activity, and host dynamics.
Notes: Variable catalog entry based on primary publication title and Watershed Function SFA description.
Study Variables 4
Variable Roles Value Context Mappings
snowmelt phase
Seasonal watershed condition relative to snowmelt timing.
Study Design Independent Variable Date Time Variable Grouping
Categorical
Entity watershed soil sampling event
Time series yes
Fields snowmelt_phase, season, collection_date

MIxS collection date MIXS:0000011
soil water content
Soil moisture context associated with snowmelt-driven viral and host dynamics.
Covariate Measured Variable
Numeric
Entity soil water
Matrix watershed soil
BERVO total soil water content BERVO:0000301
MIxS water content MIXS:0000185
viral community composition
Viral sequence profile used to quantify virus diversity in watershed soils.
Study Design Dependent Variable Measured Variable
Array
Entity soil viral community
Matrix high-altitude watershed soil
Method metagenomics
Fields viral_contig_abundance, viral_community_profile, virome_profile
viral activity
Expression or activity signal used to infer seasonal viral activity.
Study Design Dependent Variable Measured Variable
Array
Entity soil viral community
Matrix high-altitude watershed soil
Method metatranscriptomics
Fields viral_transcript_abundance, viral_activity_profile, expression_profile
Key Publications 3
doi:10.2136/vzj2018.03.0061 The East River, Colorado, Watershed: A Mountainous Community Testbed for Improving Predictive Understanding of Multiscale Hydrological-Biogeochemical Dynamics
doi:10.1186/s40168-023-01666-z Virus diversity and activity is driven by snowmelt and host dynamics in a high-altitude watershed soil ecosystem
  • Snowmelt drives seasonal pulses in viral activity linked to host population dynamics
doi:10.1021/acs.est.4c05109 Seasonal Controls on Microbial Depolymerization and Oxidation of Organic Matter in Floodplain Soils
  • Floodplain microbiomes mediate biogeochemical changes at watershed scale
Datasets 2
East River Watershed Data
Repository: DOE Data Explorer
Watershed Function SFA ESS-DIVE Collection

Comprehensive data collection from East River watershed studies

Repository: ESS-DIVE
URL: https://data.ess-dive.lbl.gov/portals/watershed-function-sfa
Collaborators: >450 individuals from 30+ institutions, 7 countries
WaDE Watershed Dynamics and Evolution (WaDE) Science Focus Area

WaDE advances predictive understanding of how dominant processes controlling watershed hydro-biogeochemical function operate under different hydrologic regimes and vary along stream networks draining heterogeneous land covers. Organized around Dynamic Headwaters, Stream Corridor Processes, Network Function, and Virtual Watershed themes.

Lead institution: Oak Ridge National Laboratory
Partners:
Colorado School Of Mines Kansas State University Oregon State University University Of Colorado At Boulder University Of Florida Virginia Tech
Funding: DOE BER / Environmental System Science (ESS)
Links:
Main Doe Page Publications
Scientific questions:
  • How do dominant processes vary under different hydrologic regimes?
  • What controls hydro-biogeochemical function along stream networks?
  • How does land cover heterogeneity affect watershed function?
  • How do biogeochemical processes respond to variable saturation in non-perennial channels?
Keywords:
Watershed Dynamics Tennessee River Basin Stream Networks Land Cover Hydro Biogeochemistry Mercury
Data types:
Water Chemistry Environmental Sensor Data Biogeochemical Data Hydrological Data
Field Sites 1
Tennessee River Basin Watersheds

Three mid-order watersheds broadly representative of the Tennessee River Basin, studied successively over 9-year plan. Most intensively used freshwater resource region in contiguous US, supporting ~4.5 million people with >280,000 gallons/day/sq mi withdrawals.

Programs: Watershed Dynamics and Evolution (WaDE) Science Focus Area
Key Publications 2
doi:10.5194/bg-22-995-2025 Reviews and syntheses: Variable inundation across Earth's terrestrial ecosystems
  • Synthesizes research on water dynamics across ecosystem types
doi:10.1029/2023WR036706
  • Subsurface water exchange dynamics vary with bedform morphology
RC-SFA River Corridor Hydrobiogeochemistry Science Focus Area

The RC-SFA delivers predictive understanding of how perturbations impact watershed hydro-biogeochemistry across the hillslope-to-stream continuum. Focus on wildfire and stream drying as interacting perturbations with uncertain impacts.

Lead institution: Pacific Northwest National Laboratory
Funding: DOE BER / Environmental System Science (ESS) (FY 2025-2028)
Links:
Main Doe Page Ideas Watersheds Github Ess Dive
NMDC umbrella study: nmdc:sty-11-x4aawf73
KBase Collection: GROW
Scientific questions:
  • How do precipitation and temperature control hydrologic connectivity?
  • What are impacts of wildfires on watershed hydro-biogeochemistry?
  • How does stream drying affect biogeochemical processes?
  • What controls carbon and nutrient transport in river corridors?
Keywords:
River Corridors Yakima River Basin Wildfire Stream Drying Hydrologic Connectivity Whondrs
Data types:
Water Chemistry Environmental Sensor Data Biogeochemical Data Geospatial
Field Sites 1
Yakima River Basin (Washington State)

Single-basin testbed with varied environmental features

Programs: River Corridor Hydrobiogeochemistry Science Focus Area
Studies 2
HIGH nmdc:sty-11-aygzgv51 - Riverbed Sediment Microbial Communities from the Columbia River (James Stegen)

Molecular-scale processes governing biogeochemical function of subsurface groundwater-surface water mixing zones (hyporheic zone). Columbia River samples from areas with varied vegetation density for studying biogeochemical hotspots.

Data modalities:
Metagenomics Metabolomics Proteomics
Primary reference: doi:10.1371/journal.pone.0228165 - Distinct temporal diversity profiles for nitrogen cycling genes in a hyporheic microbiome
Study Design
Design type: study design OBI:0500000
Observational units: Columbia River hyporheic zone sediment samples
Sampling strategy: Riverbed sediment sampling in groundwater-surface water mixing zones across locations with different vegetation density and repeated temporal observations.
Notes: Variable catalog entry based on NMDC study metadata and primary publication.
Study Variables 19
Variable Roles Value Context Mappings
vegetation density
Riparian vegetation density associated with the riverbed sediment sampling location.
Study Design Independent Variable Covariate Grouping
Categorical
Entity river corridor sampling location
Fields vegetation_density, vegetation_cover, riparian_vegetation_density

sediment sampling depth

Numeric

Matrix riverbed sediment

HIGH Functional Microbiome Catalogue from North American Rivers (Kelly Wrighton)

Catalog of 2,000+ microbial genomes from ~100 rivers across North America. Over 1,000 metagenomes sequenced through JGI from WHONDRS water samples. Landmark study published in Nature 2024.

Data modalities:
Metagenomics Metatranscriptomics
Primary reference: doi:10.1038/s41586-024-08240-z - A functional microbiome catalogue crowdsourced from North American rivers
Study Design
Design type: study design OBI:0500000
Observational units: river water samples
Sampling strategy: Crowdsourced WHONDRS river sampling across approximately 100 North American rivers with metagenomic and metatranscriptomic sequencing.
Replication: More than 1,000 metagenomes contributing to a river microbiome genome catalogue.
Notes: Variable catalog entry based on the primary Nature publication and WHONDRS/GROW description.
Study Variables 4
Variable Roles Value Context Mappings
river system
North American river or river reach represented by each WHONDRS sample.
Spatial Index Grouping
Categorical
Entity river system
Fields river, river_system, site, reach
MIxS local environmental context MIXS:0000013
collection date
Sampling date for WHONDRS river water microbiome collections.
Date Time Variable
Datetime
Entity sampling event
Fields collection_date, sampling_date, date
MIxS collection date MIXS:0000011
river microbiome genome catalogue
Metagenome-assembled genome catalogue derived from WHONDRS river metagenomes.
Study Design Dependent Variable Derived
Array
Entity river microbial genomes
Matrix river water
Method metagenome assembly and genome binning
Fields mag_id, genome_bin, genome_catalogue
microbial functional expression profile
Metatranscriptomic profile used to relate microbial functions to river corridor conditions.
Study Design Dependent Variable Measured Variable
Array
Entity river microbial community
Matrix river water
Method metatranscriptomics
Fields transcript_abundance, expression_profile, functional_expression_profile
MIxS environmental medium MIXS:0000014
Analyses & KBase Narratives 3
GROWdb US River Systems - Samples

Sample metadata and links for GROWdb US river systems. Part of the Genome Resolved Open Watersheds (GROW) database built from WHONDRS worldwide river sampling campaign.

Type: Kbase Narrative
Samples: 178
Output types:
Metagenomics
GROWdb Metagenome Assembled Genomes (MAGs)

Metagenome-assembled genomes from WHONDRS river samples. Includes MAG assemblies, quality metrics, and GTDB taxonomy assignments.

Type: Kbase Narrative
Output types:
Metagenomics
GROWdb Metabolic Models

Genome-scale metabolic models built from GROW MAGs using KBase ModelSEED pipeline. Enables prediction of metabolic capabilities and community interactions.

Type: Kbase Narrative
Output types:
Metagenomics
Key Publications 6
doi:10.1038/s41586-024-08240-z A functional microbiome catalogue crowdsourced from North American rivers
  • Over 2,000 microbial genomes catalogued from ~100 rivers across North America
  • WHONDRS provides globally scalable framework for river microbiome research
doi:10.1029/2023WR036023 Sinuosity-Driven Hyporheic Exchange: Hydrodynamics and Biogeochemical Potentials
  • River sinuosity drives hyporheic exchange affecting biogeochemical processes
doi:10.3390/w16010171 Quantifying drivers of methane hydrobiogeochemistry in a tidal river floodplain system
  • Multiple drivers control methane cycling in tidal river floodplains
doi:10.1029/2023gl106220
  • Soil mineral composition linked to hyporheic zone DOM chemistry across CONUS
doi:10.1016/j.soilbio.2024.109364 Thermodynamic control on the decomposition of organic matter across different electron acceptors
  • Thermodynamic constraints control organic matter decomposition pathways
doi:10.1021/acs.est.3c10826 Experimental open air burning of vegetation enhances organic matter chemical heterogeneity compared to laboratory burns
  • Wildfire-derived organic matter differs significantly from lab-burned material
Datasets 1
WHONDRS Community Data Packages

Community-contributed data packages from WHONDRS (Worldwide Hydrobiogeochemistry Observation Network for Dynamic River Systems).

Repository: ESS-DIVE
Mercury SFA Biogeochemical Transformations at Critical Interfaces in a Mercury Perturbed Watershed

The Mercury SFA provides foundational insight on exchange and feedback processes at critical interfaces controlling mercury fate and transformation. Research elucidates mechanisms by which inorganic mercury transforms into methylmercury at the sediment-water interface.

Lead institution: Oak Ridge National Laboratory
Funding: DOE BER / Environmental System Science (ESS)
Start date: 2010
End date: 2023
Established: 2010
Principal investigators:
Scott Brooks
Oak Ridge National Laboratory
Links:
Main Doe Page Data Page
Scientific questions:
  • What mechanisms control mercury transformation to methylmercury?
  • How do sediment-water interfaces mediate mercury cycling?
  • What microorganisms are responsible for mercury methylation?
  • How does mercury fate interact with carbon and nutrient cycling?
Keywords:
Mercury Methylmercury Biogeochemistry Sediment Water Interface Mercury Methylation Hgca Hgcb
Data types:
Water Chemistry Hydrological Data Genomics Mercury Speciation
Field Sites 1
East Fork Poplar Creek (Oak Ridge, Tennessee)

Mercury-contaminated stream system

Contamination source: Historical nuclear material research and production
Programs: Biogeochemical Transformations at Critical Interfaces in a Mercury Perturbed Watershed
Studies 2
HIGH East Fork Poplar Creek Sediment Metagenomes (Mircea Podar)

Metagenome-assembled genomes of novel prokaryotic species from mercury-contaminated East Fork Poplar Creek sediments. 28 MAGs reconstructed including Hg methylators.

Organism: sediment metagenome
Data modalities:
Metagenomics
BioProjects: PRJNA670906
Primary reference: doi:10.1128/MRA.00153-21 - Metagenome-Assembled Genome Sequences of Novel Prokaryotic Species from the Mercury-Contaminated East Fork Poplar Creek, Oak Ridge, Tennessee, USA
Study Design
Design type: study design OBI:0500000
Observational units: mercury-contaminated stream sediment samples
Sampling strategy: Sediment metagenome sampling from East Fork Poplar Creek to reconstruct MAGs and identify mercury methylation potential.
Notes: Variable catalog entry based on primary publication and study metadata.

MEDIUM Archaeal Mercury Methylators in Freshwater Sediments (Dwayne Elias)

Study demonstrating robust mercury methylation across diverse methanogenic archaea in freshwater sediments from East Fork Poplar Creek.

Organism: sediment metagenome
Data modalities:
Metagenomics Amplicon 16s
Primary reference: doi:10.1128/mBio.02403-17 - Robust Mercury Methylation across Diverse Methanogenic Archaea
Study Design
Design type: study design OBI:0500000
Observational units: methanogenic archaeal cultures, freshwater sediment microbial samples
Sampling strategy: Comparative evaluation of mercury methylation across methanogenic archaea and sediment communities.
Notes: Variable catalog entry based on primary publication title and Mercury SFA description.

doi:10.1038/s42003-020-1047-5
doi:10.1016/j.scitotenv.2019.07.001 Dissolved organic matter reduces the effectiveness of sorbents for mercury removal

East Fork Poplar Creek Discharge Data

Multi-year discharge records at Kilometer 5.4

ORNL Compiled Mercury Methylator Database

Custom reference database of hgcA and hgcB genes from mercury-methylating microorganisms (301 hgcA, 239 hgcB sequences)

Repository: DOE Data Explorer
DOI: dataset/1569274
Hg-MATE-Db

Hg-cycling Microorganisms in Aquatic and Terrestrial Ecosystems Database for identifying mercury-cycling microbes

Repository: Smithsonian Institution
DOI: 10.25573/serc.13105370.v1
Wetland Hydro-biogeochemistry Science Focus Area

This SFA investigates wetland hydro-biogeochemistry at a riparian wetland at DOE's Savannah River Site. Research aims to understand and predict wetland ecosystem function and control on carbon, nutrient, and contaminant transformations and transport.

Lead institution: Argonne National Laboratory
Partners:
Savannah River National Laboratory University Of Georgia (Savannah River Ecology Laboratory) Bulgarian Academy Of Science Illinois Institute Of Technology
Funding: DOE BER / Environmental System Science (ESS)
End date: 2024
Principal investigators:
Ken Kemner
Principal Investigator
Argonne National Laboratory
Co-investigators:
Edward O'Loughlin
Co-PI
Argonne National Laboratory
Pamela Weisenhorn
Argonne National Laboratory
Maxim Boyanov
Bulgarian Academy of Science
Dan Kaplan
Savannah River National Laboratory
John Seaman
Savannah River Ecology Laboratory, University of Georgia
Carlo Segre
Illinois Institute of Technology
Links:
Doe Page Anl Page
Scientific questions:
  • How do wetlands control elemental transformations and transport?
  • What processes regulate carbon and nutrient cycling in wetlands?
  • How do contaminants transform in wetland environments?
  • What role does hydrology play in wetland biogeochemistry?
Keywords:
Wetland Biogeochemistry Riparian Wetland Savannah River Site Carbon Cycling Nutrient Cycling Contaminant Transport
Field Sites 1
Savannah River Site Riparian Wetland (Aiken, South Carolina)

Riparian wetland representative of many in humid regions of the Southeast US

Programs: Wetland Hydro-biogeochemistry Science Focus Area
Floodplain Hydro-Biogeochemistry Science Focus Area

The Floodplain Hydro-Biogeochemistry SFA investigates how subsurface interfaces mediate molecular-scale biogeochemical processes and groundwater quality. Research focuses on microbial and geochemical responses to hydrological processes in floodplain systems.

Lead institution: SLAC National Accelerator Laboratory
Funding: DOE BER / Environmental System Science (ESS)
End date: 2024
Principal investigators:
Scott Fendorf
Stanford University / SLAC
Links:
Main Ssrl Page Doe Page
Scientific questions:
  • How do subsurface interfaces mediate biogeochemical processes?
  • What controls groundwater quality in floodplain systems?
  • How do wet-dry cycles affect redox processes and carbon mobilization?
  • What mechanisms control colloid generation and transport?
Keywords:
Floodplain Subsurface Interfaces Groundwater Quality Redox Cycling Colloid Transport Capillary Fringe
Data types:
Groundwater Chemistry Biogeochemical Data Structural Biology Economic Modeling Data
Field Sites 2
Riverton Floodplain (Wyoming)
Programs: Floodplain Hydro-Biogeochemistry Science Focus Area
Crested Butte Floodplain (Colorado)

Alluvial aquifer underlying Slate River with fine-grained soil overlying cobble/gravel alluvium (3600 m² interface)

Programs: Floodplain Hydro-Biogeochemistry Science Focus Area
Key Publications 1
doi:10.1038/ngeo2940 Thermodynamically controlled preservation of organic carbon in floodplains
Wetland Function SFA Terrestrial Wetland Function and Resilience Science Focus Area

Develops mechanistic understanding of how climatic conditions affect terrestrial wetland ecosystem function, particularly carbon dynamics. Organized around three themes: fine-scale hydro-biogeochemical mechanisms controlling C cycling and GHG emissions, above/belowground processes and interactions, and model integration at fine and intermediate scales.

Lead institution: Argonne National Laboratory
Partners:
Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory Bulgarian Academy Of Sciences University Of Minnesota University Of Nevada U.s. Geological Survey
Funding: DOE BER / Environmental System Science (ESS)
Principal investigators:
Roser Matamala
Principal Investigator
Argonne National Laboratory
Links:
Doe Page
Scientific questions:
  • How do climatic conditions affect wetland ecosystem function?
  • What mechanisms control carbon cycling and GHG emissions in wetland soils?
  • How do above and belowground processes interact in wetlands?
  • How can we integrate mechanistic understanding into ESM land components?
Keywords:
Wetland Resilience Carbon Cycling Nutrient Cycling Environmental Change
Data types:
Biogeochemical Data Water Chemistry Environmental Sensor Data
Field Sites 3
Upper Colorado River Basin - East River (Colorado)

Headwater system within the Upper Colorado River Basin

Programs: Terrestrial Wetland Function and Resilience Science Focus Area
Taylor River Watershed (Colorado)

Expansion site within Upper Colorado River Basin

Programs: Watershed Function Science Focus Area, Terrestrial Wetland Function and Resilience Science Focus Area, Colorado Headwaters Ecological Spectroscopy Study
Cottonwood Lake Study Area (North Dakota)

Depressional wetland complex in the Prairie Pothole Region

Programs: Terrestrial Wetland Function and Resilience Science Focus Area
BGC-Interfaces BioGeoChemistry at Interfaces Science Focus Area

Identifies and quantifies biogeochemical processes and underlying mechanisms controlling actinide mobility to reliably predict and control cycling and migration of actinides in the environment. Focus on plutonium (>2200 metric tons deposited globally) and neptunium behavior, including interactions with lanmodulin and similar compounds.

Lead institution: Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory
Funding: DOE BER / Environmental System Science (ESS)
End date: 2024
Principal investigators:
Mavrik Zavarin
Principal Investigator
Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory
Annie B. Kersting
Former PI / Director of University Relations
Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory
Links:
Doe Page
Scientific questions:
  • What biogeochemical processes control actinide mobility?
  • How do actinides interact with biological systems?
  • What mechanisms control plutonium migration in subsurface environments?
  • What is the role of lanmodulin in actinide chemistry?
Keywords:
Actinides Biogeochemistry Interfaces Environmental Systems Uranium Colloid Transport Contaminant Transport
Data types:
Biogeochemical Data Stable Isotope Data
Field Sites 1
Savannah River Site - Pond B (South Carolina)

Field site for actinide biogeochemistry studies

Programs: BioGeoChemistry at Interfaces Science Focus Area
User Facilities & Data Resources (5)
JGI DOE Joint Genome Institute

JGI provides integrated high-throughput sequencing, DNA design and synthesis, metabolomics, and computational analysis enabling systems-based scientific approaches. JGI sequences and analyzes genomes for DOE-relevant research in bioenergy, carbon cycling, and environmental microbiome science.

Lead institution: Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory
Funding: DOE BER / Genomic Science Program
Links:
Main Phytozome
Services:
Genome Sequencing Metagenome Sequencing Dna Synthesis Metabolomics Computational Analysis Single-Cell Genomics
Capabilities 4
OPERATIONAL High-Throughput Sequencing

Short- and long-read sequencing platforms producing billions of reads for genomic assembly, resequencing, RNAseq annotation, and metagenomics. Total output in 2024 was 870 Tb.

Type: Sequencing
Throughput: 870 Tb annually
Instruments:
  • Illumina NovaSeq - Short-read sequencing producing paired-end 150bp reads
  • PacBio Revio - Long-read sequencing averaging 60-70 kbp reads
Applications:
Genome Assembly Metagenome Sequencing Rna-Seq Single-Cell Genomics
Data products:
Raw Sequence Reads Assembled Genomes Annotated Transcripts
OPERATIONAL DNA Synthesis Platform

DNA design and synthesis capabilities supporting researchers exploring conversion of sequence into functional assessments. Minimum request is 100 kb.

Type: Dna Synthesis
Applications:
Biosynthetic Pathway Refactoring Gene Synthesis Construct Assembly
Data products:
Synthesized Dna Constructs Verified Plasmids
OPERATIONAL Metabolomics Program

Functional annotation of genomes through metabolomics, providing improved understanding of the role of small molecules in diverse biological systems. Minimum 22 samples required.

Type: Metabolomics Ms
Applications:
Secondary Metabolite Identification Biosynthetic Gene Cluster Characterization Pathway Validation
Data products:
Metabolite Profiles Mass Spectra (Submitted To Massive)
OPERATIONAL AI-Centric Analysis

AI/ML-based computational analysis including protein family discovery, biosynthetic gene cluster prediction, and genome annotation. Doubled known protein families using AI clustering algorithms.

Type: Ai Ml Analysis
Applications:
Protein Family Discovery Bgc Prediction Metagenome Analysis
Flagship Genomes 6
Populus trichocarpa (Poplar / Black Cottonwood) - 2006
First tree genome sequenced
Panicum virgatum (Switchgrass)
Candidate biofuel feedstock used by all BRCs
Miscanthus
Perennial grass bioenergy feedstock
Brachypodium distachyon
Model grass
Setaria italica (Foxtail millet)
Model C4 grass
Panicum hallii
Diploid relative of switchgrass
EMSL Environmental Molecular Sciences Laboratory

EMSL provides advanced analytical capabilities for environmental and biological research, including mass spectrometry, NMR, electron microscopy, and high-performance computing.

Lead institution: Pacific Northwest National Laboratory
Funding: DOE BER
Links:
Main
Services:
Mass Spectrometry Nmr Spectroscopy Electron Microscopy High-Performance Computing Imaging Molecular Characterization
Capabilities 6
OPERATIONAL Metabolomics Mass Spectrometry Suite

Comprehensive metabolomics workflow with 24 instruments for unbiased metabolite and lipid measurement including GC-MS, Orbitrap, and FTICR-MS platforms. Includes a 21 Tesla FTICR (one of two in the world).

Type: Metabolomics Ms
Instruments:
  • GC-MS Instruments (4) - Gas chromatography-mass spectrometry
  • Orbitrap Mass Spectrometers (16) - Diverse Orbitrap instruments including hybrid quadrupole and Tribrid
  • FTICR Mass Spectrometers (4)
  • Triple Quadrupole MS (9) - For sensitive targeted quantification and metabolic flux measurements
  • Imaging Mass Spectrometers (4) - Dedicated to mass spectrometry imaging
Applications:
Untargeted Metabolomics Targeted Metabolite Quantification Lipidomics Metabolic Flux Analysis
Data products:
Metabolite Profiles Lipid Profiles Metabolite Identifications
OPERATIONAL NMR Spectroscopy

Robust solid- and liquid-state NMR capabilities for structural biology, metabolomics, natural products, and organic matter characterization. Non-destructive with minimal sample preparation.

Type: Nmr
Instruments:
  • Liquid-State NMR - For metabolomics and natural products
  • Solid-State NMR - For structural biology and materials
Applications:
Metabolomics Structural Biology Natural Products Organic Matter Characterization
Data products:
Nmr Spectra Molecular Structure Data
OPERATIONAL Cryogenic Electron Microscopy

Cryo-TEM suite including Krios G3i cryogenic TEM and Aquilos 2 cryo-FIB-SEM for de novo structure determination, high-resolution cellular imaging, and tomography. Supports samples from 50 kDa proteins to whole cells.

Type: Cryo Em
Instruments:
  • Krios G3i - Cryogenic transmission electron microscope for high-resolution imaging
  • Aquilos 2 Cryo-FIB-SEM - Sample preparation via lamella generation with ~10 nm resolution
Applications:
De Novo Protein Structure Determination Cellular Architecture Imaging Plant-Microbe Interaction Studies Biofilm Morphology Cryo-Electron Tomography
Data products:
3d Structures Tomographic Reconstructions Cellular Ultrastructure Images
OPERATIONAL Anaerobic Microbial Phenotyping Platform (AMP2)

World's largest autonomous-capable science system for anaerobic microbial experimentation. Commissioned December 2024 by DOE Secretary Chris Wright. 18 instruments housed in 1,800 sq ft across seven anaerobic pods connected by robotic track system. Enables complete design-build-test-learn cycles with minimal operator intervention. Generates AI/ML-ready datasets in publicly available databases hosted on EMSL Science Central. Built in partnership with Ginkgo Bioworks under $4.66M contract.

Type: Anaerobic Phenotyping
Instruments:
  • Anaerobic Chambers (7) - Interconnected pods maintaining anaerobic environment
  • Liquid Handlers - Automated sample preparation, dispensing, and cherry-picking
  • Cultivation Systems - Supports up to 14 deep well plates for microbial growth
  • Fluorescence/Absorbance Readers - Optical density, fluorescence, and luminescence measurements
  • Flow Cytometers - Cell counting and characterization
  • HPLC Systems - Liquid chromatography for metabolite analysis
  • Electroporator - Cell transformation capabilities
Applications:
Anaerobic Microbe Characterization Design-Build-Test-Learn Cycles For Microbial Phenotyping High-Throughput Screening Under Variable Conditions Genotype-Phenotype Mapping Biofuel And Biomaterial Development Chemical And Energy Production From Microbes
Data products:
Growth Curves Metabolite Profiles Flow Cytometry Data Ai/ml-Ready Phenotype Datasets
OPERATIONAL NanoSIMS Imaging

Nanoscale secondary ion mass spectrometry for subcellular-resolution chemical imaging and isotope ratio analysis in biological samples.

Type: Nanosims Imaging
Applications:
Subcellular Metabolic Imaging Isotope Probing Element Mapping
OPERATIONAL Molecular Observation Network (MONet)

Open science network building a continental-scale database of standardized molecular and microstructural soil data. Integrates FTICR-MS for organic matter composition, X-ray CT for 3D pore structure, and metagenomics via JGI partnership. Data available on Science Central without embargo. See nmdc_sfas_brcs:monet for full program details.

Type: Fticr Ms
Applications:
Continental-Scale Soil Characterization Soil Organic Matter Molecular Analysis Soil Carbon Cycling Research Earth System Model Integration
Data products:
Fticr-Ms Organic Matter Profiles X-Ray Ct Pore Network Metrics Metagenome Sequences And Annotations Soil Biogeochemistry Measurements
KBase DOE Systems Biology Knowledgebase

KBase is an open-source software and data platform enabling data sharing, integration, and analysis of microbes, plants, and their communities. Provides genomic, metabolic modeling, and multi-omics analysis capabilities.

Lead institutions:
Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory Argonne National Laboratory Brookhaven National Laboratory Oak Ridge National Laboratory
Funding: DOE BER / Genomic Science Program
Links:
Main Documentation Doe Page
Capabilities 3
OPERATIONAL Genome Annotation Services

Functional annotation tools including RAST and KEGG-based annotation, plus DRAM for metabolic profiling. Machine learning classifiers predict growth phenotypes from functional annotations.

Type: Genome Annotation
Applications:
Genome Functional Annotation Mag Annotation Viral Genome Annotation (Dram-V) Phenotype Prediction
Data products:
Annotated Genomes Functional Summaries Kegg Mappings
OPERATIONAL Metabolic Modeling Pipeline

Suite of apps for genome-scale metabolic model reconstruction, analysis, and prediction. Uses OMEGGA for omics-enabled global gapfilling. Over 500 media formulations available. Recent improvements achieve 72% model accuracy.

Type: Metabolic Modeling
Applications:
Genome-Scale Model Reconstruction Flux Balance Analysis Growth Media Optimization Essential Gene Prediction Pathway Analysis
Data products:
Metabolic Models Fba Solutions Growth Predictions
OPERATIONAL Metagenome Analysis

Metagenome assembly, binning, and annotation workflows. Applied to build models for over 2,000 MAGs from the GROW (Genome Resolved Open Watersheds) project.

Type: Metagenome Analysis
Applications:
Metagenome Assembly Genome Binning Community Metabolic Modeling
Data products:
Assembled Metagenomes Mags Community Models
Reference data: Microbial genomes: >90,000 from RefSeq
Plant genomes: >50 from Phytozome
Biolog media: >300 formulations
Supported data types:
Reads Contigs Genomes Metabolic Models Growth Media Rna-Seq Expression Data Growth Phenotype Data Flux Balance Analysis Solutions
ESS-DIVE Environmental System Science Data Infrastructure for a Virtual Ecosystem

ESS-DIVE is DOE's data archive for Environmental System Science research data. Provides open access to datasets from DOE-funded environmental research including watershed, soil, and ecosystem studies.

Lead institution: Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory
Funding: DOE BER / Environmental System Science
Links:
Main
NMDC National Microbiome Data Collaborative

NMDC is a pilot initiative to advance microbiome science by building community-driven standards and infrastructure for microbiome data sharing, integration, and analysis.

Lead institutions:
Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory Los Alamos National Laboratory Pacific Northwest National Laboratory Oak Ridge National Laboratory
Funding: DOE BER
Links:
Main
DOE AI Projects (8)
OPAL Orchestrated Platform for Autonomous Laboratories to Accelerate AI-Driven BioDesign

OPAL is a BER/ASCR-supported effort to connect autonomous laboratories, robotics, AI agents, and shared data infrastructure for AI-driven biodesign. The project spans protein engineering, microbial genome-to-function mapping, and adaptive plant phenotyping across multiple DOE national laboratories.

Lead institution: Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory
Partners:
Argonne National Laboratory Oak Ridge National Laboratory Pacific Northwest National Laboratory
Funding: DOE Office of Science / Genomic Science Program AI Pilot Projects
Initiative: DOE Genomic Science AI Pilot Projects
Start date: 2025
DOE offices: BER, ASCR
Principal investigators:
Paul Adams
Principal Investigator
Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory
David Weston
Principal Investigator
Oak Ridge National Laboratory
Dion Antonopoulos
Principal Investigator
Argonne National Laboratory
Kristin Burnum-Johnson
Principal Investigator
Pacific Northwest National Laboratory
Links:
Main Award List
Scientific questions:
  • How can agentic AI and autonomous laboratories accelerate design-build-test-learn cycles in biodesign?
  • How can shared data architecture connect distributed biological experiments across DOE laboratories?
Keywords:
Machine Learning Technology Platform Synthetic Biology Biosystems Design Omics Methods
Data types:
Genomics Proteomics Metabolomics Phenotypic Data
Modalities:
Robotic Automation Computational Modeling Multi Omics High Throughput Phenotyping
LAMBDA Lakehouse-enabled AI-ready Multi-modal Bioimaging Data Architecture

LAMBDA is a cross-facility data architecture project for BER-supported structural biology and imaging resources. It will establish common data structures, metadata standards, APIs, and AI-ready workflows for multimodal bioimaging and structural biology datasets in the BER Data Lakehouse.

Lead institution: Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory
Partners:
Argonne National Laboratory Brookhaven National Laboratory Oak Ridge National Laboratory Pacific Northwest National Laboratory Slac National Accelerator Laboratory
Funding: DOE Office of Science / Genomic Science Program AI Pilot Projects
Initiative: DOE Genomic Science AI Pilot Projects
Start date: 2025
DOE offices: BER, ASCR

Scientific questions:
  • How can BER structural biology and imaging data be standardized for AI-ready reuse?
  • How can multimodal bioimaging datasets be integrated across DOE facilities?
Keywords:
Machine Learning Technology Platform Omics Methods Systems Biology
Data types:
Structural Biology Imaging Data
Modalities:
Bioimaging Synchrotron Methods Nmr Spectroscopy Computational Modeling
IDeA Intelligent Design Assistant for Enzyme Discovery and Biosynthetic Pathway Optimization

IDeA is an AI assistant project for enzyme discovery and biosynthetic pathway optimization, aimed at accelerating design of enzymes and pathways relevant to cleaner and more efficient biomanufacturing.

Lead institution: Argonne National Laboratory
Funding: DOE Office of Science / Genomic Science Program AI Pilot Projects
Initiative: DOE Genomic Science AI Pilot Projects
Start date: 2025
DOE offices: BER
Principal investigators:
Arvind Ramanathan
Principal Investigator
Argonne National Laboratory

Scientific questions:
  • How can AI-guided design accelerate enzyme discovery?
  • How can biosynthetic pathways be optimized using AI-assisted reasoning and design?
Keywords:
Machine Learning Technology Platform Synthetic Biology Metabolic Engineering
Data types:
Genomics Enzyme Activity Data
Modalities:
Computational Modeling High Throughput Screening
RESCUE Rare Earth Self-Driving Cellular Uptake Engineering

RESCUE is an AI pilot project focused on self-driving cellular engineering for rare earth uptake, connecting biological design with DOE priorities in critical minerals and biomanufacturing.

Lead institution: Brookhaven National Laboratory
Funding: DOE Office of Science / Genomic Science Program AI Pilot Projects
Initiative: DOE Genomic Science AI Pilot Projects
Start date: 2025
DOE offices: BER
Principal investigators:
Qun Liu

Brookhaven National Laboratory

  • How can self-driving experimental systems improve cellular uptake of rare earth elements?
  • What biological design strategies can support critical mineral recovery?

Machine Learning Technology Platform Synthetic Biology Biosystems Design
Data types:
Genomics Phenotypic Data
Modalities:
Robotic Automation Computational Modeling High Throughput Screening
BERIL BER Integrative Layer: Integrated AI Infrastructure for Genome-to-Phenotype Research Using the BER Data Lakehouse

BERIL is an agentic AI infrastructure project for genome-to-phenotype discovery using the BER Data Lakehouse. It is intended to orchestrate specialized reasoning agents, literature search, tool execution, and Data Lakehouse queries for BER-relevant biomanufacturing and bioproduct targets.

Lead institution: Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory
Funding: DOE BER / Genomic Science Program AI Pilot Projects
Initiative: DOE Genomic Science AI Pilot Projects
Start date: 2025
DOE offices: BER
Principal investigators:
Paramvir Dehal
Principal Investigator
Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory

Main Award List
Scientific questions:
  • How can an agentic AI layer orchestrate reasoning over BER Data Lakehouse resources?
  • How can genome-to-phenotype discovery be accelerated through AI-assisted data integration?

Machine Learning Technology Platform Omics Methods Systems Biology
Data types:
Genomics Phenotypic Data
Modalities:
Computational Modeling Multi Omics
GPTgp A Generative Pretrained Transformer for Genomic Photosynthesis to Support Abundant Bioenergy Production

GPTgp is a generative pretrained transformer project for genomic photosynthesis, focused on supporting abundant bioenergy production through AI-enabled analysis of photosynthetic systems.

Lead institution: Oak Ridge National Laboratory
Funding: DOE Office of Science / Genomic Science Program AI Pilot Projects
Initiative: DOE Genomic Science AI Pilot Projects
Start date: 2025
DOE offices: BER

Lianhong Gu

  • How can AI improve prediction and design for bioenergy production traits?

Machine Learning Bioenergy Feedstock Crop Omics Methods

Genomics Phenotypic Data

Computational Modeling Genome Sequencing
Accelerating Biological Discovery by Enabling Genomic Resources in Deep Phenotyping on Automated Platforms

This AI pilot project enables genomic resources in deep phenotyping on automated platforms, supporting faster biological discovery through AI-ready phenotype and genomic data integration.

AIMS-Leaf AI-Driven Multimodal Imaging for Predicting Stress Response in Plants: Linking Genotype to Phenotype across Scales

AIMS-Leaf is an AI pilot project for multimodal imaging to predict plant stress response and connect genotype to phenotype across scales.

Other Initiatives (8)
CHESS Colorado Headwaters Ecological Spectroscopy Study

CHESS is a coordinated airborne remote sensing and field observation campaign in the Upper Gunnison Basin, Colorado. The 2025 campaign paired NEON Airborne Observation Platform imaging spectroscopy and LiDAR flights with ground observations of vegetation composition, foliar traits, forest demography, subsurface properties, and surface water conditions across the Upper East River, Almont Triangle, and Upper Taylor Basin domains.

Lead institution: Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory
Partners:
Slac National Accelerator Laboratory Rocky Mountain Biological Laboratory Nasa Jet Propulsion Laboratory National Ecological Observatory Network
Funding: DOE Office of Science BER; NASA / Environmental System Science; EMIT Extended Mission Phase E Science
Initiative: Watershed Function SFA collaborative field campaign
Funding period: 2025
Start date: 2025-06-10
End date: 2025-07-18
DOE offices: BER
Links:
Ess Dive Data Page
Scientific questions:
  • How can airborne imaging spectroscopy and LiDAR be calibrated with field observations to infer ecosystem function in headwater watersheds?
  • How do vegetation, soil, and water interactions vary across Upper Gunnison Basin headwater environments?
Keywords:
Watershed Hydrology Upper Colorado River Basin Mountain Hydrology Drought Biogeochemistry Plant Productivity
Data types:
Remote Sensing Geospatial Phenotypic Data Hydrological Data Biogeochemical Data Imaging Data
Modalities:
Remote Sensing Field Experiments Field Sensors Stream Sampling High Throughput Phenotyping
Field Sites 2
East River Watershed (Colorado)

Mountainous community testbed in Upper Colorado River Basin, representative of vulnerable headwater systems

Programs: Watershed Function Science Focus Area, Colorado Headwaters Ecological Spectroscopy Study
Taylor River Watershed (Colorado)

Expansion site within Upper Colorado River Basin

Programs: Watershed Function Science Focus Area, Terrestrial Wetland Function and Resilience Science Focus Area, Colorado Headwaters Ecological Spectroscopy Study
Datasets 5
CHESS ESS-DIVE Data Portal

Portal collecting datasets from the Colorado Headwaters Ecological Spectroscopy Study campaign.

Repository: ESS-DIVE
URL: https://data.ess-dive.lbl.gov/portals/chess
Data type: remote sensing and field observations
Site and endmember spectra of terrestrial vegetation and soils for CHESS

Site and endmember spectra collected during the June-July 2025 CHESS campaign to validate NEON airborne hyperspectral observations and augment spectral libraries for bare surfaces, non-photosynthetic vegetation, photosynthetic vegetation, and flowers.

Repository: OSTI
URL: https://www.osti.gov/biblio/2997555
DOI: 10.15485/2997555
Data type: spectral observations
CHESS 2025: Location data for field observations and sampling

Geolocation data for CHESS field observations and sampling, including meadow, shrub, tree, stem, and Leaf Area Index plot locations collected with high-precision GNSS receivers and field mapping tools.

Repository: OSTI
URL: https://www.osti.gov/biblio/3022418
DOI: 10.15485/3022418
Data type: geospatial observations
CHESS 2025: Leaf Area Index (LAI) for meadow, shrub, tree, and understory vegetation

Leaf Area Index measurements collected across meadow, shrub, tree, and aspen understory vegetation within 72 hours of NEON AOP airborne data collection, including processed summary tables and supporting raw/intermediate files.

Repository: OSTI
URL: https://www.osti.gov/biblio/3022242
DOI: 10.15485/3022242
Data type: vegetation canopy observations
CHESS 2025: Field-collected vegetation attributes and site photos

Field observations of CHESS vegetation samples and sites, including meadow cover, shrub and tree attributes, species lists, voucher identifiers, and site photographs collected in coordination with NEON AOP airborne observations.

Repository: OSTI
URL: https://www.osti.gov/biblio/3014404
DOI: 10.15485/3014404
Data type: vegetation field observations
COMPASS-FME Coastal Observations, Mechanisms, and Predictions Across Systems and Scales - Field, Measurements, and Experiments

COMPASS-FME is a BER ESS coastal systems project improving predictive understanding of terrestrial-aquatic interfaces in the Chesapeake Bay and Western Lake Erie Basin. The project combines field observations, in situ sensors, large campaign-based collections, laboratory incubations, water-level manipulations, molecular characterization, and multiscale modeling of soil, water, sediment, microbial, and plant processes.

Lead institution: Pacific Northwest National Laboratory
Partners:
Argonne National Laboratory Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory Los Alamos National Laboratory Oak Ridge National Laboratory University Of Toledo Smithsonian Environmental Research Center
Funding: DOE BER / Environmental System Science
Initiative: COMPASS
DOE offices: BER
Principal investigators:
Vanessa Bailey
Principal Investigator
Pacific Northwest National Laboratory
Links:
Main Doe Page Ess Dive
Scientific questions:
  • What ecological, biogeochemical, and hydrological control points govern coastal terrestrial-aquatic interfaces?
  • How do flood frequency, duration, depth, and salinization affect carbon oxidation and coupled processes?
  • Which mechanisms and interactions are transferable across coastal systems and model scales?
Keywords:
Wetland Science Biogeochemistry Watershed Science Carbon Cycling Redox Cycling Hydro Biogeochemistry Environmental Systems
Data types:
Environmental Sensor Data Hydrological Data Biogeochemical Data Water Chemistry Metagenomics Metabolomics Geospatial
Modalities:
Field Experiments Field Sensors Stream Sampling Groundwater Monitoring Multi Omics Ecosystem Modeling
Field Sites 2
Chesapeake Bay Coastal Terrestrial-Aquatic Interfaces (Chesapeake Bay region, United States)

COMPASS-FME coastal terrestrial-aquatic interface study region used to compare saltwater coastal inundation, soil-water-sediment-microbe-vegetation interactions, and hydrologic disturbance responses.

Type: Coastal Terrestrial Aquatic Interface
PI: Vanessa Bailey
Institution: Pacific Northwest National Laboratory
Relevance:
Coastal Systems Wetland Ecology Hydro Biogeochemistry
Programs: Coastal Observations, Mechanisms, and Predictions Across Systems and Scales - Field, Measurements, and Experiments
Western Lake Erie Basin Coastal Terrestrial-Aquatic Interfaces (Western Lake Erie Basin, United States)

COMPASS-FME freshwater coastal terrestrial-aquatic interface study region used to compare Great Lakes hydrologic disturbance, water-level changes, soil-water-sediment-microbe-vegetation interactions, and coastal ecosystem state change.

Type: Coastal Terrestrial Aquatic Interface
PI: Vanessa Bailey
Institution: Pacific Northwest National Laboratory
Relevance:
Coastal Systems Wetland Ecology Hydro Biogeochemistry
Programs: Coastal Observations, Mechanisms, and Predictions Across Systems and Scales - Field, Measurements, and Experiments
Studies 1
Coastal Terrestrial-Aquatic Interface Flooding and Salinization Experiments (Vanessa Bailey)

Field and laboratory investigation of flooding, salinization, and water-level manipulation effects on coastal terrestrial-aquatic interface ecosystem state, redox chemistry, carbon oxidation, soil-water-sediment-microbe-plant interactions, and greenhouse gas production.

Organism: coastal soil, sediment, microbial, and plant communities
Data modalities:
Environmental Sensor Data Hydrological Data Biogeochemical Data Metagenomics Metabolomics
Source: Coastal Observations, Mechanisms, and Predictions Across Systems and Scales - Field, Measurements, and Experiments
DOE ESS program page describing COMPASS-FME research questions, study regions, datasets, and data portal.
Study Design
Design type: study design OBI:0500000
Experimental units: field water-level manipulation plots, soil and sediment incubation cores, batch experiments
Observational units: coastal terrestrial-aquatic interface samples, in situ sensor time series, soil, water, sediment, microbial, and plant measurements
Sampling strategy: Synoptic and temporally resolved sampling across shoreline-to-upland gradients, paired with field manipulations, laboratory incubations, and model integration.
Blocking: Chesapeake Bay and Western Lake Erie Basin regions; open-water, wetland, and upland gradient positions.
Notes: Variable catalog entry based on DOE ESS COMPASS-FME program description.
Study Variables 9
Variable Roles Value Context Mappings
coastal study region
COMPASS-FME region or site network represented by each sample or time series.
Grouping Spatial Index
Categorical
Levels Chesapeake Bay, Western Lake Erie Basin
Entity coastal terrestrial-aquatic interface
Fields region, site, watershed
terrestrial-aquatic interface position
Position along the open-water, wetland, shoreline, and upland coastal gradient.
Study Design Independent Variable Spatial Index
Categorical
Levels open water, wetland, shoreline, upland forest
Entity coastal gradient position
Fields transect_position, gradient_position, env_local_scale
MIxS local environmental context MIXS:0000013
flooding regime
Frequency, duration, depth, or source of flooding applied or observed in coastal TAI plots.
Experimental Factor Study Design Independent Variable
Numeric
Units cm or days
Entity flooding disturbance
Fields flood_frequency, flood_duration, flood_depth, water_level
MIxS experimental factor MIXS:0000008
salinity condition
Salinity or salinization treatment in freshwater and saltwater coastal TAI experiments.
Experimental Factor Covariate
Numeric
Units practical salinity units
Entity water chemistry
Matrix soil water or surface water
Fields salinity, conductivity, salinization_treatment
MIxS experimental factor MIXS:0000008
sample collection date
Date or timestamp for each field collection, sensor observation, or incubation sampling point.
Date Time Variable Covariate
Datetime
Entity sampling event
Time series yes
Fields collection_date, timestamp, date
geographic location
Coordinates or named location of the coastal TAI sample or sensor.
Spatial Index Covariate
Text
Entity sample location
Fields lat_lon, geo_loc_name, site_location
redox chemistry
Redox state or redox-active chemical measurements used to characterize aerobic to anaerobic shifts.
Study Design Dependent Variable Measured Variable
Numeric
Entity soil, sediment, or water redox chemistry
Matrix soil, sediment, or water
Fields redox_potential, dissolved_oxygen, iron_redox, manganese_redox
carbon oxidation and greenhouse gas flux
Carbon oxidation, carbon dioxide production, methane production, or greenhouse gas flux response to hydrologic and redox changes.
Study Design Dependent Variable Measured Variable
Numeric
Entity coastal carbon cycling
Matrix soil, sediment, water, or atmosphere
Time series yes
Fields carbon_oxidation, co2_flux, methane_flux, greenhouse_gas_flux
microbial community composition
Molecular profile of microbial communities in coastal soil, sediment, water, or plant-associated samples.
Study Design Dependent Variable Measured Variable
Array
Entity coastal microbial community
Matrix soil, sediment, water, or plant-associated material
Method sequencing or molecular characterization
Fields microbial_community, metagenome_profile, amplicon_profile, taxonomic_profile
Datasets 1
COMPASS ESS-DIVE Data Portal

ESS-DIVE portal for COMPASS-FME datasets, including in situ sensor data, campaign field collections, community science data, synthesis datasets, and chemical, biochemical, molecular, water, soil, sediment, microbial, and plant sample characterizations.

Repository: ESS-DIVE
URL: https://data.ess-dive.lbl.gov/portals/compass
Data type: coastal systems field observations and experiments
Dataset Variables 2
Variable Roles Value Context Mappings
in situ sensor observation
Automated soil, water, and plant sensor measurements available through the COMPASS portal.
Measured Variable
Array
Entity coastal TAI sensor network
Time series yes
Fields sensor_observation, timestamp, water_level, soil_moisture
discrete sample characterization
Chemical, biochemical, molecular, microbial, or plant characterization of collected coastal samples.
Measured Variable
Array
Entity coastal TAI sample
Fields chemistry_measurement, molecular_characterization, microbial_profile, plant_trait
SABI Sorghum-Anthracnose-Biocontrol Interactions

BRaVE project investigating molecular interactions between sorghum (a bioenergy crop), its anthracnose-disease causative fungal pathogen Colletotrichum sublineola (Cs), and antifungal biocontrol bacteria to create disease-resilient bioenergy crops. Uses computational and experimental approaches to study plant-pathosystem interactions.

Lead institution: Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory
Funding: DOE BER / Biopreparedness Research Virtual Environment (BRaVE)
Initiative: BRaVE
Funding period: 2023-2026
Start date: 2023
End date: 2026
DOE offices: BER, ASCR
Principal investigators:
Trent Northen
Principal Investigator
Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory
Links:
Doe Page
Scientific questions:
  • What molecular mechanisms underlie sorghum-Colletotrichum pathogenic interactions?
  • How do biocontrol bacteria protect sorghum from anthracnose disease?
  • How can we engineer disease-resilient bioenergy crops?
Keywords:
Biosecurity Sorghum Fungal Pathogens Bioenergy Crops Plant Microbe Interactions Biodefense
Data types:
Metagenomics Transcriptomics Metabolomics
Phenotype assays:
Plant Colonization Bacterial Fungal Interaction Assay Growth Curves Competition Assays
Isolate Collections 2
Sorghum-Associated Bacterial Collection

Bacterial isolates with biocontrol activity against Colletotrichum sublineola, collected from sorghum rhizosphere and phyllosphere.

Organism types:
Bacteria
Source environments:
Sorghum Rhizosphere Sorghum Phyllosphere
Host organisms: Sorghum bicolor
Colletotrichum Pathogen Collection

Colletotrichum sublineola isolates causing anthracnose disease in sorghum.

Organism types:
Fungi
Source environments:
Infected Sorghum Tissue
PlantPath Unlocking the Molecular Basis of Plant-Pathogen Interactions to Create Resilient Bioenergy Crops

BRaVE project integrating systems biology, biomolecular characterization, and synthetic biology with computation and AI/ML to understand plant-pathogen coevolution and develop disease-resistant bioenergy crops. Uses ESMBind AI model to predict protein structure and function. Studies sorghum interactions with Colletotrichum sublineola fungal pathogen and antifungal biocontrol bacteria.

Lead institution: Brookhaven National Laboratory
Partners:
Pacific Northwest National Laboratory Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory Argonne National Laboratory University Of Illinois, Urbana-Champaign Texas A&m University University Of North Carolina, Chapel Hill
Funding: DOE BER / Biopreparedness Research Virtual Environment (BRaVE)
Initiative: BRaVE
Funding period: 2023-2026
Start date: 2023
End date: 2026
DOE offices: BER, ASCR
Principal investigators:
Qun Liu
Principal Investigator
Brookhaven National Laboratory
Links:
Doe Page
Scientific questions:
  • What virulence effector proteins interact with plant immune receptors?
  • What molecular mechanisms underlie fungal pathogenicity in bioenergy crops?
  • How can synthetic infections be used to study resistance and biocontrol?
  • How do AI/ML approaches accelerate understanding of plant-pathogen interactions?
Keywords:
Biosecurity Biodefense Sorghum Fungal Pathogens Bioenergy Crops Plant Microbe Interactions Machine Learning Systems Biology
Data types:
Genomics Transcriptomics Proteomics

4D-EpiGen Decoding Host-Pathogen Dynamics with 4D (Epi)Genomics

BRaVE project developing methods to understand how hosts survive pathogenic attacks through epigenetic mechanisms. Creates an exascale computational and explainable artificial intelligence workflow to map genome and epigenome changes during infection. Integrates 3D genomic maps, epigenetic modifications, and cryo-electron microscopy imaging to validate structure-function relationships and enable predictive dynamic modeling of host-pathogen dynamics.

Lead institution: Los Alamos National Laboratory
Partners:
Brookhaven National Laboratory Oak Ridge National Laboratory University Of Arizona Harvard University
Funding: DOE BER / Biopreparedness Research Virtual Environment (BRaVE)
Initiative: BRaVE
Funding period: 2023-2026
Start date: 2023
End date: 2026
DOE offices: BER, ASCR
Principal investigators:
Shawn Starkenburg
Principal Investigator
Los Alamos National Laboratory
Links:
Doe Page
Scientific questions:
  • How do epigenetic mechanisms help hosts survive pathogenic attacks?
  • What genome structural changes are induced by viral pathogens?
  • How can we characterize molecular signatures of infection in genomes and epigenomes?
  • Can AI workflows enable predictive 4D modeling of host-pathogen dynamics?
Keywords:
Biosecurity Biodefense Systems Biology Machine Learning Virome
Data types:
Genomics Transcriptomics Imaging Data
CyanoPath Enhancing Biopreparedness Through a Model System to Understand Molecular Mechanisms of Pathogenesis

NW-BRaVE (Northwest BRaVE) project at PNNL developing a powerful and user-friendly platform to elucidate the fundamental principles of how molecular interactions drive pathogen-host relationships. Uses cyanobacteria (including Prochlorococcus marinus MED4) and their viral parasites (cyanophages) as a model system. Integrates structural biology (cryo-ET), genomics, proteomics, and multiomics measurements with machine learning to study pathogen adaptation and coevolution across molecular and evolutionary scales. Key computational tools include Lattice Microbes (spatiotemporal modeling), PTM-Psi (post-translational modification analysis), and EcoKMER (data processing). Research thrusts span cryo-ET visualization of molecular interactions, multiomics regulatory pathway mapping, spatiotemporal dynamics of cyanobacteria-phage systems, and multimodal data integration for host-pathogen surveillance.

Lead institution: Pacific Northwest National Laboratory
Partners:
Oak Ridge National Laboratory Argonne National Laboratory University Of Colorado Anschutz Medical Campus University Of Illinois, Urbana-Champaign Washington State University University Of Houston Texas Southern University Colorado Department Of Public Health And Environment
Funding: DOE BER / Biopreparedness Research Virtual Environment (BRaVE)
Initiative: BRaVE
Funding period: 2023-2026
Start date: 2023
End date: 2026
DOE offices: BER, ASCR
Principal investigators:
Margaret S. Cheung
Principal Investigator
Pacific Northwest National Laboratory
Links:
Doe Page Main
Scientific questions:
  • What molecular complexes form during pathogen infection?
  • How do regulatory changes occur during infection at the proteomics level?
  • What evolutionary factors affect host-pathogen coevolution?
  • How can we integrate multi-scale data for biosurveillance?
  • How do redox-dependent post-translational modifications regulate the dark complex and carbon fixation?
Keywords:
Biosecurity Biodefense Microbial Ecosystems Virome Phage Machine Learning Co Evolution
Data types:
Genomics Proteomics Transcriptomics Imaging Data Structural Biology

Cyanophage P-HM2 AMG Metabolic Flux Analysis in MED4 (Margaret S. Cheung)

Genome-scale metabolic model studying synergy and antagonism of cyanophage P-HM2 auxiliary metabolic gene (AMG) impacts in Prochlorococcus marinus MED4. Demonstrates that 17 directly hijacked reactions substantially impact over 30% of metabolism including carbon fixation, photosynthesis, and nucleotide synthesis. First integration of AMGs and phage assembly into a genome-scale metabolic model.

Organism: Prochlorococcus marinus MED4
Data modalities:
Genomics
Primary reference: doi:10.1101/2024.12.11.628001 - Synergy and antagonism in a genome-scale model of metabolic hijacking by bacteriophage
Study Design
Design type: study design OBI:0500000
Observational units: genome-scale model reactions, simulated phage-host metabolic states
Sampling strategy: Genome-scale metabolic modeling of Prochlorococcus MED4 metabolic hijacking by cyanophage P-HM2 auxiliary metabolic genes.
Notes: Variable catalog entry based on study description and primary reference.

Fields scenario, phage_infection, AMG_condition

Cyanophage P-HM2 AMG represented in the metabolic model.

Identifier

Fields AMG, phage_gene, gene_id

Numeric
Entity host metabolic flux
Method genome-scale metabolic modeling
Fields flux, flux_prediction, pathway_impact
Systematic Scale-Up and Enhanced Purification of Cyanophage P-SSP7 (James E. Evans)

Methodology for scaling P-SSP7 cyanophage production from lab volumes to 40-liter scale with enhanced purification. Achieves phage titers exceeding 3e12 units/mL with recoverable yields of 1e13 total infectious units and 60-fold reduction in cryo-EM data collection time by increasing usable particles per field of view.

Organism: Prochlorococcus marinus MED4
Data modalities:
Structural Biology
Primary reference: doi:10.64898/2026.01.03.697218 - Systematic Scale-Up and Enhanced Purification of Marine Cyanophage P-SSP7

Observational units: phage purification batches, cryo-EM particle preparations
Sampling strategy: Scale-up and purification method development for P-SSP7 cyanophage production from lab volumes to 40-liter scale.
Notes: Variable catalog entry based on study description and primary reference.

S. elongatus PCC 7942 Redox Proteome Under Light Disturbance (Margaret S. Cheung)

Systems approach examining redox activities and conformational changes of proteins and nucleic acid interactions in multi-layered protein-DNA regulatory networks under light disturbance in Synechococcus elongatus PCC 7942. Identifies significant shifts in cysteine redox states in regulatory proteins including transcription sigma factors and metabolic enzymes.

Organism: Synechococcus elongatus PCC 7942
Data modalities:
Proteomics Transcriptomics
Primary reference: doi:10.48550/arXiv.2410.09346 - Transcriptome and Redox Proteome Reveal Temporal Scales of Carbon Metabolism Regulation in Model Cyanobacteria Under Light Disturbance

Experimental units: Synechococcus elongatus PCC 7942 cultures
Observational units: redox proteomics samples, transcriptomics samples
Sampling strategy: Time-resolved transcriptome and redox proteome profiling of S. elongatus under light disturbance.
Notes: Variable catalog entry based on study description and primary reference.

4D Whole-Cell Model of Prochlorococcus marinus MED4 (Margaret S. Cheung)

Spatiotemporal 4D (3D + time) whole-cell model of Prochlorococcus marinus MED4 representing the spatial organization of enzymatic and molecular processes in central carbon metabolism under light perturbation. Uses cryo-ET at 10 nm resolution as structural constraints. Demonstrates that redox-dependent post-translational modifications regulate the structural assembly of the "dark complex" (a protein megacomplex of Gap, CP12, and Prk), modulating metabolic flux at a conserved regulatory node of the Calvin-Benson cycle. Built using the Lattice Microbes platform. First spatiotemporal simulation of a photosynthetic autotroph.

Organism: Prochlorococcus marinus MED4
Data modalities:
Imaging Data Proteomics Structural Biology
Primary reference: doi:10.64898/2026.01.21.700212 - Spatiotemporal 4D Whole-cell Modeling of a Minimal Autotroph Reveals Central Carbon Metabolism Regulated Locally by Protein Megacomplexes via Post-translational Modifications under Light Disturbance
Study Design
Design type: time series design OBI:0500020
Observational units: simulated whole-cell model states, cryo-ET structural constraints, metabolic flux predictions
Sampling strategy: Spatiotemporal whole-cell simulation constrained by cryo-ET and proteomic data to model Prochlorococcus carbon metabolism under light perturbation.
Notes: Variable catalog entry based on study description and primary reference.

Numeric

Fields flux, metabolic_flux, CBC_flux
Key Publications 1
doi:10.1002/pro.4822 PTM-Psi: A python package to facilitate the computational investigation of post-translational modification on protein structures and their impacts on dynamics and functions
Datasets 2
Lattice Microbe Prochlorococcus Whole-Cell Model Integration Workflow

Computational workflow simulating the 4D intracellular environment of Prochlorococcus marinus MED4 (CCMP1986). Includes cell boundary, thylakoid, ribosomes, DNA structures, genome-scale metabolic network, spatially resolved transcription/translation/diffusion for 15 key genes, and light-driven post-translational modification of CBC enzymes controlling dark complex formation. PNNL-SA-219756.

Repository: Zenodo
URL: https://zenodo.org/records/18025765
DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.18025765
GRIP-Tomo: GRaph Identification of Proteins in Tomograms

Pipeline for identifying and classifying structures in cryo-ET tomography data using global and local topological (network) features. Converts PDB structures and 3D density data to graphs for feature-based classification. Used for cryo-ET analysis in NW-BRaVE.

Repository: Zenodo
URL: https://zenodo.org/records/17127842
DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.17127842
PhageFoundry Phage Foundry: High-Throughput Platform for Rapid Design of Countermeasures Against Drug-Resistant Pathogens

BRaVE project developing an integrated platform combining multiscale characterization of phage-host molecular interactions with high-throughput isolation, phage-host coevolution studies, machine learning, and engineering design principles. Creates a comprehensive knowledgebase for rapid development of bacteriophage therapeutics against antimicrobial-resistant bacterial infections. Enables scalable approaches for emerging drug-resistant pathogens.

Lead institution: Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory
Partners:
Sandia National Laboratories University Of California, Berkeley San Francisco State University University Of Chicago University Of Texas Medical Branch
Funding: DOE BER / Biopreparedness Research Virtual Environment (BRaVE)
Initiative: BRaVE
Funding period: 2023-2026
Start date: 2023
End date: 2026
DOE offices: BER, ASCR
Principal investigators:
Vivek Mutalik
Principal Investigator
Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory
Links:
Doe Page
Scientific questions:
  • How can we characterize phage-host interactions at multiple scales?
  • What machine learning approaches can predict phage therapeutic effectiveness?
  • How can we rapidly isolate and engineer phages against emerging pathogens?
  • What are the principles of phage-host coevolution?
Keywords:
Biosecurity Biodefense Phage Virome Microbial Ecosystems Machine Learning Co Evolution Synthetic Biology
Data types:
Genomics Proteomics Metagenomics

E. coli Phage Resistance Landscape (Vivek Mutalik)

High-throughput mapping of the phage resistance landscape in E. coli using gain-of-function, loss-of-function, and gene deletion libraries. Generates datasets for predictive models of phage-mediated selection on bacterial phenotype and evolution.
